document: Data Protection Act DPA 2018 (United Kingdom - Row 6)

sections.heading: Overview
sections.title: Overview of personal data processing scope
sections.summary: This section establishes that the Act governs personal data processing. Most processing activities fall under the UK GDPR. Part 2 supplements the UK GDPR, Part 3 covers processing by competent authorities, Part 4 addresses intelligence services, Part 5 defines the Information Commissioner's role, Part 6 handles enforcement, and Part 7 provides supplementary provisions including Crown and Parliament applications.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets the foundational scope for data processing activities under the Act, clarifying which parts apply to different contexts like competent authorities and intelligence services. It establishes the framework for understanding where data transfer impact assessments would be relevant.

requirements.text: Most processing of personal data is subject to the UK GDPR
Part 2 supplements the UK GDPR
Part 3 makes provision about processing by competent authorities
Part 4 makes provision about processing by intelligence services
Part 5 makes provision about the Information Commissioner
Part 6 makes provision about enforcement of data protection legislation
Part 7 makes supplementary provision including Crown and Parliament applications

sections.source_quote: This Act makes provision about the processing of personal data. Most processing of personal data is subject to the [F1UK GDPR]. Part 2 supplements the [F2UK GDPR]. Part 3 makes provision about the processing of personal data by competent authorities. Part 4 makes provision about the processing of personal data by the intelligence services [F4(and certain processing carried out by competent authorities jointly with the intelligence services)]. Part 5 makes provision about the Information Commissioner. Part 6 makes provision about the enforcement of the data protection legislation. Part 7 makes supplementary provision, including provision about the application of this Act to the Crown and to Parliament.

sections.heading: Protection of personal data
sections.title: Personal data protection framework
sections.summary: This section states that the UK GDPR and this Act protect individuals through lawful and fair processing based on consent or other specified bases. It grants data subjects rights to obtain information about processing and request corrections for inaccurate data. It also establishes the Commissioner's role in monitoring and enforcing these provisions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the core protections for personal data, including consent requirements and data subject rights, which directly impact how data transfers must be structured to comply with legal bases and data subject rights.

requirements.text: The UK GDPR and this Act protect individuals with regard to the processing of personal data
Personal data must be processed lawfully and fairly, on the basis of the data subject's consent or another specified basis
Data subjects have the right to obtain information about the processing of personal data
Data subjects have the right to require inaccurate personal data to be rectified
The Commissioner has functions to monitor and enforce provisions

sections.source_quote: The [F5UK GDPR] and this Act protect individuals with regard to the processing of personal data, in particular by—requiring personal data to be processed lawfully and fairly, on the basis of the data subject's consent or another specified basis, conferring rights on the data subject to obtain information about the processing of personal data and to require inaccurate personal data to be rectified, and conferring functions on the Commissioner, giving the holder of that office responsibility for monitoring and enforcing their provisions.

sections.heading: Terms relating to the processing of personal data
sections.title: Definitions of key data terms
sections.summary: This section defines critical terms including personal data, identifiable living individual, processing, data subject, controller, processor, filing system, and the Commissioner. It specifies that personal data relates to identified or identifiable living individuals and processing encompasses various operations on information. The Commissioner is defined as the Information Commissioner.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides essential definitions that directly impact data transfer impact assessments by clarifying what constitutes personal data, processing activities, and the roles of controllers and processors in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: Personal data means any information relating to an identified or identifiable living individual
Identifiable living individual means a living individual who can be identified, directly or indirectly
Processing means an operation or set of operations performed on information
Data subject means the identified or identifiable living individual to whom personal data relates
Controller and processor have the same meaning as in Part 2, Part 3 or Part 4
Filing system means any structured set of personal data accessible according to specific criteria
The Commissioner means the Information Commissioner

sections.source_quote: (1) This section defines some terms used in this Act. (2) "Personal data" means any information relating to an identified or identifiable living individual (subject to subsection (14)(c)). (3) "Identifiable living individual" means a living individual who can be identified, directly or indirectly, in particular by reference to—(a) an identifier such as a name, an identification number, location data or an online identifier, or (b) one or more factors specific to the physical, physiological, genetic, mental, economic, cultural or social identity of the individual. (4) "Processing", in relation to information, means an operation or set of operations which is performed on information, or on sets of information, such as—(a) collection, recording, organisation, structuring or storage, (b) adaptation or alteration, (c) retrieval, consultation or use, (d) disclosure by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making available, (e) alignment or combination, or (f) restriction, erasure or destruction, (subject to subsection (14)(c) and sections 5(7), 29(2) and 82(3), which make provision about references to processing in the different Parts of this Act). (5) "Data subject" means the identified or identifiable living individual to whom personal data relates.

sections.heading: F9
sections.title: Definitions for Part 2
sections.summary: This section defines terms used in Part 2 and the UK GDPR, specifying that terms have the same meaning in both contexts unless modified by this Part. It clarifies that references to processing in this Part apply to processing covered by this Part.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the definition framework for Part 2, which is critical for understanding how data processing activities are governed in the context of the UK GDPR and data transfer impact assessments.

requirements.text: Terms used in this Part and the UK GDPR have the same meaning in this Part as they have in the UK GDPR
The reference to a term's meaning in the UK GDPR is to its meaning in the UK GDPR read with any provision of this Part which modifies the term's meaning
Subsection (1) is subject to any provision in this Part which provides expressly for the term to have a different meaning
A reference in this Part to the processing of personal data is to processing to which this Part applies

sections.heading: Meaning of "controller"
sections.title: Definition of controller
sections.summary: This section defines the controller in the context of the UK GDPR, specifying that the controller is the person on whom the obligation to process the data is imposed by the relevant enactment, subject to subsection (2), section 209, and section 210.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies the definition of controller, which is critical for determining responsibility in data transfers and impact assessments, particularly when multiple entities are involved.

requirements.text: The definition of "controller" in Article 4(1)(7) of the UK GDPR has effect subject to subsection (2), section 209, and section 210
For the purposes of the UK GDPR, where personal data is processed only by the person on whom the obligation to process the data is imposed by the enactment

sections.source_quote: The definition of "controller" in Article [F364(1)(7)] of the UK GDPR has effect subject to—(a) subsection (2), (b) section 209, and (c) section 210. For the purposes of the UK GDPR, where personal data is processed only—(a) the person on whom the obligation to process the data is imposed by the enactment (or, if different, one of the enactments) is the controller.

sections.heading: Meaning of "public authority" and "public body"
sections.title: Definition of public authorities and public bodies
sections.summary: This section defines which entities qualify as 'public authorities' or 'public bodies' under the UK GDPR. It specifies that public authorities include entities defined by the Freedom of Information Act 2000, Scottish public authorities as defined by the Freedom of Information (Scotland) Act 2002, the Advanced Research and Invention Agency, and any authorities specified by the Secretary of State in regulations. An entity must perform tasks in the public interest or exercise official authority to qualify as a public authority for GDPR purposes. The section excludes specific local government bodies and charter trustees from being considered public authorities.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines which entities are considered public authorities for GDPR purposes, which impacts data transfer assessments involving public sector entities.

requirements.text: Public authorities include entities defined by the Freedom of Information Act 2000
Public authorities include Scottish public authorities as defined by the Freedom of Information (Scotland) Act 2002
Public authorities include the Advanced Research and Invention Agency
Public authorities include any authorities specified by the Secretary of State in regulations
An entity qualifies as a public authority only if it performs tasks in the public interest or exercises official authority

Parish councils in England, community councils in Wales, community councils in Scotland, parish meetings under the Local Government Act 1972, and community meetings under the Local Government Act 1972 are excluded from being public authorities
Charter trustees under specific sections of the Local Government Act 1972 and related regulations are excluded from being public authorities
The Secretary of State may specify entities that are not public authorities through regulations

sections.source_quote: 7. (1) For the purposes of the UK GDPR, the following (and only the following) are 'public authorities' and 'public bodies'—(a) a public authority as defined by the Freedom of Information Act 2000, (b) a Scottish public authority as defined by the Freedom of Information (Scotland) Act 2002 (asp 13), [the Advanced Research and Invention Agency], and (c) an authority or body specified or described by the Secretary of State in regulations, subject to subsections (2), (3) and (4).

sections.heading: Lawfulness of processing: public interest etc
sections.title: Public interest processing exceptions for lawfulness
sections.summary: This section specifies that processing of personal data for the administration of justice, exercise of parliamentary functions, functions conferred by law, functions of the Crown or government departments, or activities supporting democratic engagement qualifies as lawful under the UK GDPR. These exceptions are explicitly listed and apply to public authorities performing tasks in the public interest or exercising official authority.
sections.dtia_summary: Identifies specific public interest activities that may be processed without additional safeguards, relevant for data transfers involving public sector entities.

requirements.text: Processing of personal data for administration of justice is lawful under UK GDPR
Processing of personal data for exercise of parliamentary functions is lawful under UK GDPR
Processing of personal data for functions conferred by law is lawful under UK GDPR
Processing of personal data for functions of the Crown, Ministers or government departments is lawful under UK GDPR
Processing of personal data for activities supporting democratic engagement is lawful under UK GDPR

sections.source_quote: 8. In Article 6(1) of the UK GDPR, the reference in point (e) to processing of personal data that is necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority includes processing of personal data that is necessary for—(a) the administration of justice, (b) the exercise of a function of either House of Parliament, (c) the exercise of a function conferred on a person by an enactment or rule of law, (d) the exercise of a function of the Crown, a Minister of the Crown or a government department, or (e) an activity that supports or promotes democratic engagement.

sections.heading: [F48Relevant international law
sections.title: Special categories of personal data: supplementary provisions
sections.summary: This section specifies additional circumstances under which processing of special categories of personal data is lawful. It includes conditions for processing by health professionals or social work professionals, and by others who owe a duty of confidentiality under law. It also defines personal data relating to criminal convictions and offences, including alleged offenses and proceedings for offenses.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides supplementary conditions for processing special categories of personal data, relevant for data transfer impact assessments involving sensitive information.

requirements.text: Processing of special categories of personal data by health professionals or social work professionals is lawful
Processing of special categories of personal data by others who owe a duty of confidentiality under law is lawful
Personal data relating to criminal convictions includes alleged offenses and proceedings for offenses

sections.heading: Limits on fees that may be charged by controllers
sections.title: Limits on fees for data access requests
sections.summary: This section sets limits on fees that controllers may charge for data access requests under the UK GDPR. It allows the Secretary of State to specify fee limits for responses to manifestly unfounded or excessive requests, and for providing further copies of data. Controllers must publish guidance on fees if specified in regulations, and the guidance must include specific details.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines fee limits for data access requests, which may impact data transfer costs and compliance with GDPR.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State may specify limits on fees for responses to manifestly unfounded or excessive requests
The Secretary of State may specify limits on fees for providing further copies of data
Controllers must publish guidance on fees if specified in regulations

sections.source_quote: 12. (1) The Secretary of State may by regulations specify limits on the fees that a controller may charge in reliance on—(a) Article 12(5) of the UK GDPR (reasonable fees when responding to manifestly unfounded or excessive requests), or (b) Article 15(3) of the UK GDPR (reasonable fees for provision of further copies).

sections.heading: Obligations of credit reference agencies
sections.title: Credit reference agencies' data processing obligations
sections.summary: This section outlines specific obligations for credit reference agencies under the UK GDPR. It states that controllers must apply the GDPR's confirmation of processing, access to data, and third country transfer safeguards only to personal data relating to financial standing, unless the data subject has indicated a contrary intention. When disclosing data, credit reference agencies must include a statement about the data subject's rights to correct wrong information under the Consumer Credit Act 1974.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets specific data processing obligations for credit reference agencies, which is critical for data transfer impact assessments involving financial data.

requirements.text: Credit reference agencies must apply GDPR obligations only to personal data relating to financial standing
Credit reference agencies must include a statement about data subject's rights to correct wrong information when disclosing data

sections.source_quote: 13. (1) This section applies where a controller is a credit reference agency (within the meaning of section 145(8) of the Consumer Credit Act 1974). (2) The controller's obligations under Article 15(1) to (3) of the UK GDPR (confirmation of processing, access to data and safeguards for third country transfers) are taken to apply only to personal data relating to the data subject's financial standing, unless the data subject has indicated a contrary intention. (3) Where the controller discloses personal data in pursuance of Article 15(1) to (3) of the UK GDPR, the disclosure must be accompanied by a statement informing the data subject of the data subject's rights under section 159 of the Consumer Credit Act 1974 (correction of wrong information).

sections.heading: F6014
sections.title: Exemptions for GDPR rules in specific circumstances
sections.summary: This section allows exemptions from UK GDPR rules in specified circumstances, including for freedom of expression, health data, and national security. It restricts the application of Articles 13-21 and 34 of the UK GDPR in particular contexts such as health, social work, education, child abuse data, and information prohibited by law. The exemptions are subject to conditions outlined in Schedules 2-4.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides exemptions for data transfers and processing that may be necessary for health, social work, education, child abuse data, or national security purposes, requiring specific safeguards for cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: Schedules 2, 3 and 4 make provision for exemptions from, and restrictions and adaptations of the application of, rules of the UK GDPR

sections.heading: F76
sections.title: Accreditation of certification providers
sections.summary: This section outlines the accreditation process for certification providers under the UK GDPR. Accreditation must be carried out by the Commissioner or the UK national accreditation body, with specific conditions on publishing statements and not withdrawing them. Certification providers must provide information to the Secretary of State and may charge reasonable fees.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the accreditation process for certification providers that issue certifications under the UK GDPR, ensuring they meet specific standards for data protection.

requirements.text: Accreditation of a person as a certification provider is only valid when carried out by the Commissioner or the UK national accreditation body

The UK national accreditation body may only accredit a person as a certification provider where the Commissioner has published a statement and has not published a notice withdrawing that statement
The publication of a notice under subsection (2)(b) or (3)(b) does not affect the validity of any accreditation carried out before its publication
The UK national accreditation body may charge a reasonable fee in connection with its functions under this section
The UK national accreditation body must provide the Secretary of State with information relating to its functions under this section

sections.title: Definitions for FOI public authorities
sections.summary: This section defines what constitutes 'FOI public authority' for the purposes of the Freedom of Information Act 2000 and related legislation. It specifies that FOI public authorities include public authorities in England and Wales, Scottish public authorities, and the Advanced Research and Invention Agency. It also clarifies when personal data is not considered 'held' by an FOI public authority.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the scope of FOI public authorities and when personal data is considered 'held' by them, which is relevant for data transfers and processing under the UK GDPR.

requirements.text: In this Chapter, 'FOI public authority' means a public authority as defined in the Freedom of Information Act 2000, a Scottish public authority as defined in the Freedom of Information (Scotland) Act 2002, or the Advanced Research and Invention Agency

Personal data is not treated as 'held' by an FOI public authority where section 7 of the Freedom of Information Act 2000 prevents Parts 1 to 5 of that Act from applying to the personal data
Personal data is not treated as 'held' by an FOI public authority where section 7(1) of the Freedom of Information (Scotland) Act 2002 prevents that Act from applying to the personal data

sections.title: Law enforcement purposes definition
sections.summary: This section defines 'law enforcement purposes' as activities related to preventing, investigating, detecting, or prosecuting criminal offences. The definition specifies that these purposes include actions taken by competent authorities to address criminal activities. The definition does not specify which entities or jurisdictions are covered by this term.

sections.title: Key definitions for data protection
sections.summary: This section defines critical terms including 'controller', 'processor', 'employee', 'personal data breach', 'profiling', 'recipient', 'restriction of processing', and 'third country'. It specifies that a controller is the entity determining data processing purposes or the competent authority imposing processing obligations, while a processor handles data on behalf of the controller. The section also clarifies that personal data breaches involve security failures leading to unauthorized access or disclosure of personal data.

requirements.text: In this Part, "controller" means the competent authority which, alone or jointly with others—determines the purposes and means of the processing of personal data, or is the controller by virtue of subsection (2)
Where personal data is processed only the competent authority on which the obligation to process the data is imposed by the enactment is the controller
In this Part, "processor" means any person who processes personal data on behalf of the controller (other than a person who is an employee of the controller)
"Employee" includes an individual who holds a position under the direction and control of that person
"Personal data breach" means a breach of security leading to accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to, personal data
"Profiling" means automated processing of personal data to evaluate aspects relating to an individual
"Recipient" means any person to whom the data is disclosed, excluding public authorities in legal inquiries
"Restriction of processing" means marking stored personal data to limit future processing
"Third country" means a country or territory outside the United Kingdom

sections.source_quote: Meaning of "controller" and "processor" (1) In this Part, "controller" means the competent authority which, alone or jointly with others—(a) determines the purposes and means of the processing of personal data, or (b) is the controller by virtue of subsection (2). (2) Where personal data is processed only—(a) the competent authority on which the obligation to process the data is imposed by the enactment (or, if different, one of the enactments) is the controller. (3) In this Part, "processor" means any person who processes personal data on behalf of the controller (other than a person who is an employee of the controller).

sections.heading: Overview and general duty of controller
sections.title: Controller's general duty and data protection principles
sections.summary: This section outlines the six data protection principles that controllers must adhere to. It specifies that the controller is responsible for demonstrating compliance with these principles, which include lawful and fair processing, specified and legitimate purposes, adequate and relevant data, accuracy, retention limits, and secure processing. The section also notes that sections 35-42 provide detailed provisions for each principle and specific safeguards.

requirements.text: This Chapter sets out the six data protection principles as follows—(a) section 35(1) sets out the first data protection principle (requirement that processing be lawful and fair)
section 36(1) sets out the second data protection principle (requirement that purposes of processing be specified, explicit and legitimate)
section 37 sets out the third data protection principle (requirement that personal data be adequate, relevant and not excessive)
section 38(1) sets out the fourth data protection principle (requirement that personal data be accurate and kept up to date)
section 39(1) sets out the fifth data protection principle (requirement that personal data be kept for no longer than is necessary)
section 40 sets out the sixth data protection principle (requirement that personal data be processed in a secure manner)
The controller in relation to personal data is responsible for, and must be able to demonstrate, compliance with this Chapter

sections.source_quote: Overview and general duty of controller (1) This Chapter sets out the six data protection principles as follows—(a) section 35(1) sets out the first data protection principle (requirement that processing be lawful and fair); (b) section 36(1) sets out the second data protection principle (requirement that purposes of processing be specified, explicit and legitimate); (c) section 37 sets out the third data protection principle (requirement that personal data be adequate, relevant and not excessive); (d) section 38(1) sets out the fourth data protection principle (requirement that personal data be accurate and kept up to date); (e) section 39(1) sets out the fifth data protection principle (requirement that personal data be kept for no longer than is necessary); (f) section 40 sets out the sixth data protection principle (requirement that personal data be processed in a secure manner). (2) In addition—(a) each of sections 35, 36, 38 and 39 makes provision to supplement the principle to which it relates, and (b) sections 41 and 42 make provision about the safeguards that apply in relation to certain types of processing. (3) The controller in relation to personal data is responsible for, and must be able to demonstrate, compliance with this Chapter.

sections.heading: The first data protection principle
sections.title: Lawful basis for law enforcement data processing
sections.summary: This section establishes the lawful basis for processing personal data for law enforcement purposes. It requires that such processing be lawful and fair, based on either consent from the data subject or necessity for a competent authority to perform tasks related to law enforcement. For sensitive data, additional conditions apply, including the presence of an appropriate policy document as specified in section 42. The section also outlines the conditions under which sensitive processing may be permitted.

requirements.text: The processing of personal data for any of the law enforcement purposes must be lawful and fair

The processing of personal data for any of the law enforcement purposes is lawful only if and to the extent that it is based on law and either the data subject has given consent to the processing for that purpose or the processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out for that purpose by a competent authority
Where the processing for any of the law enforcement purposes is sensitive processing, the processing is permitted only in the two cases set out in subsections (4) and (5)

The first case is where the data subject has given consent to the processing for the law enforcement purpose as mentioned in subsection (2)(a) and at the time when the processing is carried out, the controller has an appropriate policy document in place (see section 42)

The second case is where the processing is strictly necessary for the law enforcement purpose, meets at least one of the conditions in Schedule 8, and at the time when the processing is carried out, the controller has an appropriate policy document in place (see section 42)
The Secretary of State may by regulations amend Schedule 8 by adding conditions or varying or omitting conditions added by regulations under paragraph (a)

sections.source_quote: The first data protection principle (1) The first data protection principle is that the processing of personal data for any of the law enforcement purposes must be lawful and fair. (2) The processing of personal data for any of the law enforcement purposes is lawful only if and to the extent that it is based on law and either—(a) the data subject has given consent to the processing for that purpose, or (b) the processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out for that purpose by a competent authority.

sections.heading: The second data protection principle
sections.title: Law enforcement data purpose specification
sections.summary: This section specifies that law enforcement data must be collected for explicit, legitimate purposes and must not be processed in a manner incompatible with the original purpose. It allows for data to be processed for other law enforcement purposes if authorized by law and necessary and proportionate, but prohibits processing for non-law enforcement purposes without legal authorization.

requirements.text: The law enforcement purpose for which personal data is collected must be specified, explicit and legitimate
Personal data so collected must not be processed in a manner that is incompatible with the purpose for which the controller collected it

Personal data collected for a law enforcement purpose may be processed for any other law enforcement purpose provided that the controller is authorized by law to process the data for the other purpose and the processing is necessary and proportionate to that other purpose
Personal data collected for any of the law enforcement purposes may not be processed for a purpose that is not a law enforcement purpose unless the processing is authorised by law

sections.source_quote: The second data protection principle (1) The second data protection principle is that—(a) the law enforcement purpose for which personal data is collected must be specified, explicit and legitimate, and (b) personal data so collected must not be processed in a manner that is incompatible with the purpose for which it is collected.

sections.heading: The third data protection principle
sections.title: Adequacy of law enforcement data
sections.summary: This section states that personal data processed for law enforcement purposes must be adequate, relevant, and not excessive in relation to the purpose for which it is processed. This principle ensures that data collected by law enforcement authorities is proportionate and necessary for the intended use, avoiding unnecessary collection of information.

requirements.text: Personal data processed for any of the law enforcement purposes must be adequate, relevant and not excessive in relation to the purpose for which it is processed

sections.source_quote: The third data protection principle The third data protection principle is that personal data processed for any of the law enforcement purposes must be adequate, relevant and not excessive in relation to the purpose for which it is processed.

sections.heading: The fourth data protection principle
sections.title: Accuracy and rectification of law enforcement data
sections.summary: Personal data used for law enforcement must be accurate and kept up to date. Unaccurate data must be erased or rectified promptly. Data must be distinguished between factual records and assessments. Separate categories of data subjects must be clearly identified, such as suspects, convicted individuals, victims, and witnesses. Inaccurate, incomplete, or outdated data must not be transmitted for law enforcement purposes. Data quality must be verified before transmission, and necessary information about accuracy, completeness, and reliability must accompany transmissions. If data errors or unlawful transmissions occur, recipients must be notified immediately.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires data accuracy and timely rectification for law enforcement data transfers. Mandates verification of data quality before transmission and notification of errors. Critical for ensuring reliable data flows in cross-border law enforcement contexts.

requirements.text: Personal data processed for law enforcement must be accurate and kept up to date
Unaccurate personal data must be erased or rectified without delay
Personal data must be distinguished between factual records and assessments
Clear distinction must be made between different categories of data subjects
Inaccurate, incomplete, or outdated data must not be transmitted for law enforcement purposes
Data quality must be verified before transmission
Necessary information about data accuracy, completeness, and reliability must be included in transmissions
Recipient must be notified without delay if data is incorrect or transmission is unlawful

sections.source_quote: Personal data processed for any of the law enforcement purposes must be accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date, and every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that is inaccurate, having regard to the law enforcement purpose for which it is processed, is erased or rectified without delay.

sections.heading: The fifth data protection principle
sections.title: Time limits for law enforcement data retention
sections.summary: Personal data used for law enforcement must be retained only as long as necessary for the specific purpose. Appropriate time limits must be established for reviewing whether data should continue to be stored. This ensures data is not kept longer than required by law enforcement objectives.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires strict time-bound retention for law enforcement data, critical for minimizing data exposure in cross-border transfers and ensuring compliance with data minimization principles.

requirements.text: Personal data processed for law enforcement must be kept for no longer than necessary for the purpose
Appropriate time limits must be established for periodic review of continued storage

sections.source_quote: Personal data processed for any of the law enforcement purposes must be kept for no longer than is necessary for the purpose for which it is processed.

sections.heading: The sixth data protection principle
sections.title: Security requirements for law enforcement data
sections.summary: Personal data used for law enforcement must be processed with appropriate security measures to protect against unauthorized or unlawful processing, accidental loss, destruction, or damage. This includes technical and organizational safeguards to ensure data integrity and confidentiality.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates robust security for law enforcement data transfers, essential for protecting sensitive information during cross-border data flows and preventing breaches.

requirements.text: Personal data processed for law enforcement must ensure appropriate security using technical or organizational measures
Appropriate security includes protection against unauthorized or unlawful processing and accidental loss, destruction, or damage

sections.source_quote: Personal data processed for any of the law enforcement purposes must be so processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (and, in this principle, "appropriate security" includes protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage).

sections.heading: Safeguards: archiving
sections.title: Archiving safeguards for law enforcement data
sections.summary: Archiving personal data for law enforcement purposes in the public interest, scientific research, or statistical purposes requires specific safeguards. Processing is prohibited if it involves individual data subjects or likely to cause substantial damage or distress. This section ensures archiving is conducted responsibly without harming individuals.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines when archiving law enforcement data is permissible and requires safeguards to prevent harm, critical for data storage in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: Archiving for law enforcement purposes in public interest, scientific research, or statistical purposes requires safeguards
Processing is prohibited if it involves individual data subjects
Processing is prohibited if likely to cause substantial damage or distress to data subjects

sections.source_quote: This section applies in relation to the processing of personal data for a law enforcement purpose where the processing is carried out for archiving purposes in the public interest, for scientific or historical research purposes, or for statistical purposes.

sections.heading: Safeguards: sensitive processing
sections.title: Sensitive processing safeguards
sections.summary: Controllers must maintain an appropriate policy document for sensitive processing, including procedures for compliance with data protection principles and retention/erasure policies. The policy must be retained, reviewed periodically, and made available to the Commissioner. Records must detail reliance on consent or conditions, compliance with lawfulness, and retention practices.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires detailed documentation for sensitive data processing, critical for ensuring compliance with data protection standards in cross-border transfers involving high-risk data.

requirements.text: Controller must produce a document explaining procedures for securing compliance with data protection principles
Controller must produce a document explaining retention and erasure policies with retention duration
Controller must retain the policy document during the relevant period
Controller must review and update the policy document periodically
Controller must make the policy document available to the Commissioner on request
Records must include whether processing is based on consent or a condition in Schedule 8
Records must detail how processing satisfies lawfulness requirements
Records must confirm compliance with retention and erasure policies

sections.source_quote: The controller has an appropriate policy document in place in relation to the sensitive processing if the controller has produced a document which explains the controller's procedures for securing compliance with the data protection principles in connection with sensitive processing in reliance on the consent of the data subject or (as the case may be) in reliance on the condition in question.

sections.heading: Overview and scope
sections.title: Data subject rights and scope
sections.summary: This chapter establishes data subject rights including access to personal data, rectification, erasure, and restriction of processing. It also covers automated decision-making and supplementary provisions. These rights apply to law enforcement data processing but exclude criminal investigations and proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines data subject rights for law enforcement data, critical for ensuring transparency and control in cross-border data transfers involving individuals.

requirements.text: Controller must make information available to data subjects
Data subjects have a right to access their personal data
Data subjects have rights to rectify, erase, or restrict processing of personal data
Automated decision-making is regulated

sections.source_quote: This Chapter imposes general duties on the controller to make information available (see sections 44 and 45A); confers a right of access by the data subject (see sections 45 and 45A); confers rights on the data subject with respect to the rectification of personal data and the erasure of personal data or the restriction of its processing (see sections 46 to 48).

sections.heading: F176... Controller's general duties
sections.title: Controller's information disclosure obligations to data subjects
sections.summary: Controllers must provide data subjects with specific information including controller identity, data protection officer contact details, processing purposes, data subject rights to access, rectification, erasure, and restriction of processing, and the right to complain to the Commissioner. This applies to all data subjects and controllers processing personal data. The information must be made available generally or through other means, and in specific cases for data subject rights, additional details about legal basis, retention periods, and recipients are required.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates detailed disclosure of data processing information to data subjects, which impacts DTIA by requiring controllers to document and communicate specific data subject rights information, including processing purposes, retention periods, and recipient categories, especially when data subjects exercise their rights.

requirements.text: Controller must provide data subject with identity and contact details
Controller must provide data subject with data protection officer contact details where applicable
Controller must provide data subject with purposes for processing personal data
Controller must inform data subject of rights to access, rectify, erase or restrict processing data
Controller must inform data subject of right to make complaint to controller
Controller must inform data subject of right to lodge complaint with Commissioner
Controller must provide data subject with contact details of Commissioner
Controller must provide data subject with legal basis for processing where necessary for rights exercise
Controller must provide data subject with retention period or criteria for determining retention period
Controller must provide data subject with categories of recipients of personal data
Controller must provide data subject with further information necessary for rights exercise
Controller may restrict information provision to data subject where necessary and proportionate for data subject's fundamental rights or legitimate interests
Controller must inform data subject in writing without undue delay if information provision is restricted

sections.source_quote: 44(1) The controller must make available to data subjects the following information (whether by making the information generally available to the public or in any other way)—(a) the identity and the contact details of the controller; (b) where applicable, the contact details of the data protection officer (see sections 69 to 71); (c) the purposes for which the controller processes personal data; (d) the existence of the rights of data subjects to request from the controller—(i) access to personal data (see section 45), (ii) rectification of personal data (see section 46), and (iii) erasure of personal data or the restriction of its processing (see section 47);

sections.heading: Right of access by the data subject
sections.title: Data subject's right to access personal data
sections.summary: Data subjects have the right to obtain confirmation whether personal data concerning them is being processed, and if so, access to that data and related information. This includes processing purposes, legal basis, categories of data, recipients, retention periods, and data subject rights to rectification, erasure, restriction, and complaints. Controllers must provide this information in writing within a specified timeframe, and may restrict disclosure where necessary for data subject's fundamental rights or legitimate interests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires controllers to provide detailed data access information to data subjects, which impacts DTIA by mandating specific disclosures about data processing purposes, retention periods, and recipient categories, especially when data subjects exercise their access rights.

requirements.text: Data subject entitled to obtain confirmation whether personal data concerning them is being processed
Data subject entitled to access personal data and information set out in subsection (2)
Information includes purposes of and legal basis for processing
Information includes categories of personal data concerned
Information includes recipients or categories of recipients to whom personal data has been disclosed
Information includes period for which personal data will be stored or criteria for determining retention period
Information includes existence of data subject's rights to request rectification of personal data
Information includes existence of data subject's right to erasure or restriction of processing
Information includes existence of data subject's right to make complaint to controller
Information includes existence of data subject's right to lodge complaint with Commissioner
Information includes contact details of Commissioner
Information must be provided in writing before end of applicable time period
Controller may restrict rights conferred by subsection (1) where necessary and proportionate for data subject's fundamental rights or legitimate interests
Controller must inform data subject in writing without undue delay if rights are restricted

sections.source_quote: 45(1) The controller must, if so requested by a data subject, rectify without undue delay inaccurate personal data relating to the data subject.

sections.heading: I45
sections.title: Data subject's right to access personal data
sections.summary: Data subjects have the right to obtain confirmation whether personal data concerning them is being processed, and if so, access to that data and related information. This includes processing purposes, legal basis, categories of data, recipients, retention periods, and data subject rights to rectification, erasure, restriction, and complaints. Controllers must provide this information in writing within a specified timeframe, and may restrict disclosure where necessary for data subject's fundamental rights or legitimate interests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires controllers to provide detailed data access information to data subjects, which impacts DTIA by mandating specific disclosures about data processing purposes, retention periods, and recipient categories, especially when data subjects exercise their access rights.

requirements.text: Data subject entitled to obtain confirmation whether personal data concerning them is being processed
Data subject entitled to access personal data and information set out in subsection (2)
Information includes purposes of and legal basis for processing
Information includes categories of personal data concerned
Information includes recipients or categories of recipients to whom personal data has been disclosed

Information includes existence of data subject's right to erasure or restriction of processing

Information includes contact details of Commissioner
