Controller must inform data subject in writing without undue delay if rights are restricted

sections.source_quote: 45(1) The controller must, if so requested by a data subject, rectify without undue delay inaccurate personal data relating to the data subject.

sections.heading: Right to rectification
sections.title: Data subject's right to rectification of personal data
sections.summary: Controllers must rectify inaccurate personal data without undue delay when requested by a data subject. If the data is incomplete, controllers must complete it. In appropriate cases, rectification may be fulfilled by providing a supplementary statement. If rectification would infringe legal obligations, controllers must instead restrict processing.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires controllers to rectify inaccurate personal data upon request, which impacts DTIA by mandating specific procedures for data rectification, including the possibility of restricting processing when rectification would infringe legal obligations.

requirements.text: Controller must rectify inaccurate personal data without undue delay when requested
Controller must complete incomplete personal data when requested
Rectification duty may be fulfilled by providing supplementary statement
Controller must restrict processing instead of rectifying data if required for evidence purposes

sections.source_quote: 46(1) The controller must, if so requested by a data subject, rectify without undue delay inaccurate personal data relating to the data subject.

sections.heading: Right to erasure or restriction of processing
sections.title: Data subject's right to erasure or restriction of processing
sections.summary: Controllers must erase personal data without undue delay where processing infringes legal obligations or the controller has a legal obligation to erase data. If erasure would infringe evidence requirements, controllers must restrict processing instead. Controllers must also restrict processing if data accuracy is contested but cannot be determined.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires controllers to erase or restrict processing of personal data upon request, which impacts DTIA by mandating specific procedures for data erasure and restriction, including the possibility of restricting processing when erasure would infringe evidence requirements.

requirements.text: Controller must erase personal data without undue delay where processing infringes legal obligations
Controller must erase personal data without undue delay where controller has legal obligation to erase data
Controller must restrict processing instead of erasing data if required for evidence purposes
Controller must restrict processing if data accuracy is contested but cannot be determined

sections.source_quote: 47(1) The controller must erase personal data without undue delay where—(a) the processing of the personal data would infringe section 35, 36(1) to (3), 37, 38(1), 39(1), 40, 41 or 42, or (b) the controller has a legal obligation to erase the data.

sections.heading: Rights under section 46 or 47: supplementary
sections.title: Supplementary rights for data subject rectification or erasure requests
sections.summary: When a data subject requests rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing, controllers must inform them in writing whether the request was granted, and if refused, the reasons for refusal, right to complain to the Commissioner, right to make complaint to controller, and right to apply to court. Controllers must provide this information within a specified timeframe and may restrict disclosure where necessary for data subject's fundamental rights or legitimate interests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires controllers to provide detailed responses to data subject rectification or erasure requests, which impacts DTIA by mandating specific disclosures about request outcomes, refusal reasons, and related rights, especially when data subjects exercise their rights.

requirements.text: Controller must inform data subject in writing whether rectification or erasure request was granted
Controller must inform data subject in writing if request was refused, including reasons for refusal
Controller must inform data subject of right to make request to Commissioner
Controller must inform data subject of right to make complaint to controller
Controller must inform data subject of right to lodge complaint with Commissioner
Controller must inform data subject of right to apply to court
Controller must provide information within applicable time period
Controller may restrict information provision to data subject where necessary and proportionate for data subject's fundamental rights or legitimate interests
Controller must inform data subject in writing without undue delay if information provision is restricted

sections.source_quote: 48(1) Where a data subject requests the rectification or erasure of personal data or the restriction of its processing, the controller must inform the data subject in writing—(a) whether the request has been granted, and (b) if it has been refused—(i) of the reasons for the refusal

sections.heading: [F20550A Automated processing and significant decisions
sections.title: Automated decisions: definition of significant and automated decisions
sections.summary: This section defines when a decision is considered 'automated' (no meaningful human involvement) and 'significant' (producing adverse legal effects or similarly significant adverse effects for the data subject). These definitions are critical for determining when automated decision-making requires additional safeguards under sections 50B and 50C.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines when automated decisions are significant, which directly impacts whether data transfers involving such decisions require specific safeguards under section 50C.

requirements.text: A decision is based solely on automated processing if there is no meaningful human involvement in the taking of the decision
A decision is a significant decision if it produces an adverse legal effect for the data subject or has a similarly significant adverse effect for the data subject

sections.source_quote: 49 (1)(a) and (b) For the purposes of sections 50B and 50C— a decision is based solely on automated processing if there is no meaningful human involvement in the taking of the decision, and a decision is a significant decision, in relation to a data subject, if— (i) it produces an adverse legal effect for the data subject, or (ii) it has a similarly significant adverse effect for the data subject.

sections.heading: Restrictions on automated decision-making based on sensitive processing
sections.title: Restrictions on automated decisions using sensitive data
sections.summary: This section prohibits automated decisions based solely on sensitive processing unless explicit consent is given or the decision is legally required. It applies to significant decisions that could impact data subjects' rights and freedoms.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts automated decisions using sensitive data without human oversight, requiring explicit consent or legal authorization for cross-border data transfers involving such decisions.

requirements.text: A significant decision based entirely or partly on sensitive processing may not be taken based solely on automated processing
The decision must be based entirely on processing of personal data to which the data subject has given explicit consent
The decision must be required or authorised by law

sections.source_quote: 50B (1) A significant decision based entirely or partly on sensitive processing may not be taken based solely on automated processing, unless one of the following conditions is met. (2) The first condition is that the decision is based entirely on processing of personal data to which the data subject has given explicit consent. (3) The second condition is that the decision is required or authorised by law.

sections.heading: Safeguards for automated decision-making
sections.title: Safeguards for automated decisions
sections.summary: This section mandates specific safeguards for significant automated decisions, including providing information to data subjects, enabling representations, allowing human intervention, and contesting decisions. These safeguards apply when decisions are based on personal data and automated processing, but exclude cases with meaningful human involvement in reconsideration.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific safeguards for automated decisions that could impact data subjects, including transparency, appeal mechanisms, and human oversight, which are critical for cross-border data transfers involving automated decision-making.

requirements.text: The controller must ensure safeguards for the data subject's rights, freedoms and legitimate interests are in place which comply with subsection (2) and any regulations under section 50D(4)
Safeguards must provide the data subject with information about decisions taken in relation to the data subject
Safeguards must enable the data subject to make representations about such decisions
Safeguards must enable the data subject to obtain human intervention on the part of the controller in relation to such decisions
Safeguards must enable the data subject to contest such decisions

Subsections (1) and (2) do not apply if exemption from those provisions is required for a reason listed in subsection (4), the controller reconsiders the decision as soon as reasonably practicable, and there is meaningful human involvement in the reconsideration of the decision

sections.source_quote: 50C (1) Subject to subsection (3), where a significant decision taken by or on behalf of a controller in relation to a data subject is— (a) based entirely or partly on personal data, and (b) based solely on automated processing, the controller must ensure that safeguards for the data subject's rights, freedoms and legitimate interests are in place which comply with subsection (2) and any regulations under section 50D(4). (2) The safeguards must consist of or include measures which— (a) provide the data subject with information about decisions described in subsection (1) taken in relation to the data subject; (b) enable the data subject to make representations about such decisions; (c) enable the data subject to obtain human intervention on the part of the controller in relation to such decisions; (d) enable the data subject to contest such decisions.

sections.heading: Further provision about automated decision-making
sections.title: Regulatory provisions for automated decision-making
sections.summary: This section allows the Secretary of State to create regulations that define meaningful human involvement in automated decisions, describe significant decisions, and specify additional safeguards for data subjects. These regulations can amend sections 50A and 50C, but require affirmative resolution.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows regulators to define when human involvement is meaningful in automated decisions and specify additional safeguards, which can impact how cross-border data transfers involving automated decisions are assessed.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State may by regulations provide that, for the purposes of sections 50A(1)(a) and 50C(3)(c), there is, or is not, to be taken to be meaningful human involvement in the taking or reconsideration of a decision

The Secretary of State may by regulations provide that, for the purposes of section 50A(1)(b)(ii), a description of decision is, or is not, to be taken to have a similarly significant adverse effect for the data subject
Regulations under subsection (1) or (2) may amend section 50A

The Secretary of State may by regulations make provisions about the safeguards required under section 50C(1) including additional measures, supplementary requirements, or measures not to satisfy subsection (2)

sections.source_quote: 50D (1) The Secretary of State may by regulations provide that, for the purposes of sections 50A(1)(a) and 50C(3)(c), there is, or is not, to be taken to be meaningful human involvement in the taking or reconsideration of a decision in cases described in the regulations.

sections.heading: Exercise of rights through the Commissioner
sections.title: Data subject rights through the Commissioner
sections.summary: This section allows data subjects to request the Commissioner to verify the lawfulness of restrictions on information provision, access rights, legal professional privilege exemptions, or refusal of data correction/erasure requests. The Commissioner must respond with a determination of lawfulness and inform the data subject of their right to court action.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a mechanism for data subjects to challenge data controller actions through the Commissioner, which is relevant for cross-border data transfers where data subjects have rights to access or correct their information.

requirements.text: Where a controller restricts information provided to the data subject under section 44(2), the data subject may request the Commissioner to check the restriction was lawful
Where a controller relies on legal professional privilege exemption, the data subject may request the Commissioner to check the controller was entitled to rely on the exemption
Where a controller refuses a request for rectification or erasure, the data subject may request the Commissioner to check the refusal was lawful
The Commissioner must take steps to respond to a request under subsection (2) and inform the data subject whether the restriction was lawful
The Commissioner must inform the data subject of their right to apply to a court under section 167

sections.source_quote: 51 (1) This section applies where a controller— (a) restricts under section 44(4) the information provided to the data subject under section 44(2) (duty of the controller to give the data subject additional information), (b) restricts under section 45(4) the data subject's rights under section 45(1) (right of access), [F206(ba) relies on the exemption from sections 44(2) and 45(1) in section 45A (legal professional privilege)], or (c) refuses a request by the data subject for rectification under section 46 or for erasure or restriction of processing under section 47.

sections.heading: Form of provision of information etc
sections.title: Form and accessibility of information provision
sections.summary: This section requires controllers to provide data subjects with information in a concise, intelligible, and easily accessible form using clear language. It mandates free provision of information, reasonable identity verification, and consistent response formats for data subject requests.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures data transfers involving information provision comply with accessibility standards, which is critical for cross-border data transfers where data subjects have rights to access their information.

requirements.text: The controller must take reasonable steps to ensure information provided to the data subject is concise, intelligible, and easily accessible using clear and plain language
Information may be provided in any form, including electronic form
Where information is provided in response to a data subject request, the controller must provide it in the same form as the request where practicable

Where the controller has reasonable doubts about the identity of an individual making a request, the controller may request additional information to confirm identity or delay dealing with the request until identity is confirmed
Any information required by this Chapter to be provided to the data subject must be provided free of charge
The controller must facilitate the exercise of the data subject's rights arising under sections 45 to 50D

sections.source_quote: 52 (1) The controller must take reasonable steps to ensure that any information that is required by [F210or under] this Chapter to be provided to the data subject is provided in a concise, intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language.

sections.heading: F210 Words in s. 52(1) inserted (5.2.2026) by Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (c. 18), s. 142(1), Sch. 6
sections.title: Handling manifestly unfounded or excessive data subject requests
sections.summary: Controllers may charge reasonable fees or refuse action on data subject requests deemed manifestly unfounded or excessive. Examples include repetitive requests. Controllers must justify such determinations in proceedings. The Secretary of State may regulate fee limits and require publishers of fee guidance. Controllers must inform data subjects of refusal reasons and complaint rights if they do not act.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines conditions for when data subject requests can be refused or fee-charged, impacting how organizations handle excessive requests without violating data subject rights.

requirements.text: Controller may charge reasonable fee for dealing with manifestly unfounded or excessive data subject requests
Controller may refuse action on manifestly unfounded or excessive data subject requests
Example of excessive request: one that merely repeats substance of previous requests
Controller bears burden of proving request is manifestly unfounded or excessive in proceedings
Secretary of State may specify limits on fees charged for manifestly unfounded or excessive requests
Controller must inform data subject of reasons for not acting on request and right to complain with Commissioner

sections.source_quote: Where a request [made by a data subject under or by virtue of any of sections 45, 46, 47, 50C or 50D] is manifestly unfounded or excessive, the controller may— (a) charge a reasonable fee for dealing with the request, or (b) refuse to act on the request.

sections.heading: Manifestly unfounded or excessive requests by the data subject
sections.title: Handling manifestly unfounded or excessive data subject requests
sections.summary: Controllers may charge reasonable fees or refuse action on data subject requests deemed manifestly unfounded or excessive. Examples include repetitive requests. Controllers must justify such determinations in proceedings. The Secretary of State may regulate fee limits and require publishers of fee guidance. Controllers must inform data subjects of refusal reasons and complaint rights if they do not act.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines conditions for when data subject requests can be refused or fee-charged, impacting how organizations handle excessive requests without violating data subject rights.

requirements.text: Controller may charge reasonable fee for dealing with manifestly unfounded or excessive data subject requests
Controller may refuse action on manifestly unfounded or excessive data subject requests
Example of excessive request: one that merely repeats substance of previous requests
Controller bears burden of proving request is manifestly unfounded or excessive in proceedings
Secretary of State may specify limits on fees charged for manifestly unfounded or excessive requests
Controller must inform data subject of reasons for not acting on request and right to complain with Commissioner

sections.source_quote: Where a request [made by a data subject under or by virtue of any of sections 45, 46, 47, 50C or 50D] is manifestly unfounded or excessive, the controller may— (a) charge a reasonable fee for dealing with the request, or (b) refuse to act on the request.

sections.heading: Meaning of "applicable time period"
sections.title: Timeframe for data subject request responses
sections.summary: The applicable time period for responding to data subject requests is one month from the latest of: receipt of request, receipt of requested information, or payment of fees. Controllers may extend this period by two months for complex or high-volume requests with prior notice. The extension must specify reasons and occur before the initial one-month period ends.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets timeframes for responding to data subject requests, critical for DTIAs to ensure timely compliance without undue delays.

requirements.text: Applicable time period means one month beginning with relevant time
Relevant time is latest of: receipt of request, receipt of requested information, payment of fees
Controller may extend applicable time period by two months for complex or high-volume requests
Extension notice must be given before end of initial one-month period
Extension notice must state reasons for delay

sections.source_quote: The applicable time period means the period of one month beginning with the relevant time, subject to subsection (3A).

sections.heading: Overview and scope
sections.title: Controller and processor obligations framework
sections.summary: This chapter establishes general obligations for controllers and processors including security measures, breach response, data protection officers, and codes of conduct. Controllers must implement appropriate technical and organizational measures considering technology, costs, processing scope, and individual rights risks. Adherence to approved codes of conduct may satisfy compliance requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides the foundational framework for controller and processor obligations, critical for DTIAs to ensure comprehensive compliance with security and governance requirements.

requirements.text: Controllers must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure processing compliance
Technical and organisational measures must consider latest technology, implementation costs, processing scope, and individual rights risks
Adherence to approved code of conduct may demonstrate compliance with this Part

sections.source_quote: Each controller must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure, and to be able to demonstrate, that the processing of personal data complies with the requirements of this Part.

sections.heading: General obligations of the controller
sections.title: Controller technical and organisational measures
sections.summary: Controllers must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure data processing compliance. These measures must be reviewed and updated as necessary. Controllers may use adherence to approved codes of conduct as evidence of compliance with this section's requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires controllers to implement and maintain security measures, directly impacting DTIAs for data processing compliance.

requirements.text: Controller must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure processing compliance
Technical and organisational measures must be reviewed and updated where necessary
Adherence to approved code of conduct may be used to demonstrate compliance

sections.source_quote: Each controller must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure, and to be able to demonstrate, that the processing of personal data complies with the requirements of this Part.

sections.heading: Data protection by design and default
sections.title: Data protection by design and default
sections.summary: Controllers must implement technical and organisational measures designed to integrate data protection principles and ensure that by default, only necessary personal data is processed. This applies to data collection, processing scope, storage duration, and accessibility. Measures must prevent data from being accessible to an indefinite number of people without individual intervention.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires data protection by design and default, critical for DTIAs to ensure minimal data processing and enhanced security.

requirements.text: Controller must implement measures designed to implement data protection principles effectively
Controller must implement measures to integrate necessary safeguards into processing
Controller must ensure by default only necessary personal data is processed for specific purposes
Measures must apply to data amount, processing extent, storage period, and accessibility
Data must not be accessible to indefinite number of people without individual intervention

sections.source_quote: Each controller must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures which are designed to implement the data protection principles in an effective manner, and to integrate into the processing itself the safeguards necessary for that purpose.

sections.heading: Joint controllers
sections.title: Joint controllers: responsibilities and contact point
sections.summary: Joint controllers, defined as competent authorities jointly determining processing purposes and means, must establish transparent arrangements to define their compliance responsibilities. This arrangement must designate a single contact point for data subjects. These arrangements cannot override existing legal determinations.
sections.dtia_summary: Joint controllers require explicit coordination mechanisms to ensure compliance with data protection obligations across jurisdictions, critical for cross-border data processing involving multiple authorities.

requirements.text: Joint controllers must determine their respective compliance responsibilities through transparent arrangements
The arrangement must designate a single contact point for data subjects

sections.source_quote: Where two or more competent authorities jointly determine the purposes and means of processing personal data, they are joint controllers for the purposes of this Part.

sections.heading: Processors
sections.title: Processor obligations and sub-processing rules
sections.summary: Controllers may engage processors who provide sufficient technical and organisational measures to comply with data protection laws. Processors must obtain explicit controller authorization before engaging sub-processors and maintain written contracts detailing processing purposes, data categories, and security obligations. Processors must act only on controller instructions and ensure data deletion or return upon contract termination.
sections.dtia_summary: Processors must adhere to strict contractual terms and authorization protocols for sub-processing, ensuring data transfers and storage comply with cross-border data protection requirements.

requirements.text: Processor must provide guarantees for sufficient technical and organisational measures
Processor may not engage sub-processors without prior written controller authorization
Processor must inform controller if adding or replacing sub-processors
Contract must specify processing purposes, data categories, and controller/processor obligations
Processor must act only on controller instructions
Processor must ensure authorized personnel have confidentiality duties
Processor must assist controller in ensuring data subject rights compliance
Processor must delete or return personal data upon contract termination
Processor must delete copies unless legally required to retain them
Processor must provide documentation demonstrating compliance with this section
Processor may transfer data to third countries only with controller instructions

sections.source_quote: The processor used by the controller may not engage another processor without the prior written authorisation of the controller.

sections.heading: Processing under the authority of the controller or processor
sections.title: Restricted processing under controller or processor authority
sections.summary: Any entity processing personal data under a controller's or processor's authority may only do so on the controller's instructions or to comply with a legal obligation. This section restricts unauthorized processing activities to ensure data processing remains lawful and within defined boundaries.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts data processing to controller or processor instructions and legal obligations, critical for ensuring data transfers and storage remain compliant with cross-border data protection frameworks.

requirements.text: Processor or person acting under controller/processor authority may not process data except on controller instructions
Processor or person acting under controller/processor authority may not process data except to comply with legal obligation

sections.source_quote: A processor, and any person acting under the authority of a controller or processor, who has access to personal data may not process the data except—(a) on instructions from the controller, or (b) to comply with a legal obligation.

sections.heading: Records of processing activities
sections.title: Records of processing activities
sections.summary: Controllers must maintain detailed records of all processing activities, including data categories, purposes, recipients, and security measures. Processors must similarly document their activities for the controller. These records must be accessible to the data protection commissioner upon request to ensure transparency and accountability.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates comprehensive documentation of data processing activities, essential for verifying compliance with cross-border data transfers and ensuring transparency in international data flows.

requirements.text: Controller must maintain records of all processing activities

Records must include controller contact details, joint controller details, data protection officer details, processing purposes, recipients, data categories, profiling use, transfers, legal basis, and erasure timelines
Processor must maintain records of processing activities for the controller
Processor records must include processor details, controller details, processing categories, transfers, and security measures
Controller and processor must make records available to the Commissioner on request

sections.source_quote: Each controller must maintain a record of all categories of processing activities for which the controller is responsible.

sections.heading: Logging
sections.title: Logging requirements for automated systems
sections.summary: Controllers and processors must maintain logs for key processing operations including collection, alteration, consultation, disclosure, combination, and erasure. Logs must enable verification of processing lawfulness, internal monitoring, security integrity, and criminal investigations. Logs must be available to the data protection commissioner upon request.
sections.dtia_summary: Detailed logging of data processing activities ensures traceability and accountability for cross-border data transfers, critical for compliance with international data protection standards.

requirements.text: Controller or processor must keep logs for collection, alteration, consultation, disclosure, combination, and erasure
Logs of consultation must show justification, date/time, and user identity
Logs of disclosure must show justification, date/time, and recipient identity
Logs may only be used for verifying lawfulness, self-monitoring, security, or criminal proceedings
Controller or processor must make logs available to the Commissioner on request

sections.source_quote: A controller (or, where personal data is processed on behalf of the controller by a processor, the processor) must keep logs for at least the following processing operations in automated processing systems—collection; alteration; consultation; disclosure (including transfers); combination; erasure.

sections.heading: Co-operation with the Commissioner
sections.title: Cooperation with data protection commissioner
sections.summary: Controllers and processors must cooperate with the data protection commissioner upon request to facilitate the commissioner's tasks. This ensures transparency and accountability in data processing activities, particularly for cross-border data transfers and international compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates cooperation with data protection authorities, critical for ensuring cross-border data transfers comply with international data protection standards.

requirements.text: Controller and processor must cooperate with the Commissioner on request

sections.source_quote: Each controller and each processor must co-operate, on request, with the Commissioner in the performance of the Commissioner's tasks.

sections.heading: Data protection impact assessment
sections.title: Mandatory data protection impact assessment for high-risk processing
sections.summary: Controllers must conduct a data protection impact assessment before processing personal data that poses high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms. The assessment must evaluate risks to data subjects and include measures to mitigate those risks. The assessment must also detail safeguards and security mechanisms to protect personal data and ensure compliance with data protection requirements. This applies to all controllers processing personal data.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires explicit assessment of high-risk processing activities before implementation, including risk analysis and mitigation strategies. Critical for identifying and addressing potential data protection vulnerabilities in high-risk operations.

requirements.text: Controller must conduct data protection impact assessment before processing personal data likely to result in high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms
Data protection impact assessment must include general description of envisaged processing operations
Data protection impact assessment must assess risks to data subjects' rights and freedoms
Data protection impact assessment must detail measures to address identified risks
Data protection impact assessment must include safeguards and security measures to protect personal data

sections.source_quote: Where a type of processing is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals, the controller must, prior to the processing, carry out a data protection impact assessment.

sections.heading: Prior consultation with the Commissioner
sections.title: Prior consultation with Commissioner for high-risk data processing
sections.summary: Controllers must consult the Information Commissioner before processing personal data that would pose high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms, as determined by a data protection impact assessment. The consultation requires submission of the impact assessment and any additional information requested by the Commissioner. The Commissioner may provide written advice within 6 weeks, with potential extension for complex cases. This applies to filing systems and data processing operations.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates pre-processing consultation with regulator for high-risk data operations, requiring detailed impact assessments and potential regulatory review before implementation.

requirements.text: Controller must consult Commissioner prior to processing if data protection impact assessment indicates high risk to individuals' rights and freedoms
Controller must provide Commissioner with data protection impact assessment and any requested information
Commissioner must provide written advice if processing would infringe data protection requirements
Commissioner must provide written advice within 6 weeks of consultation request
Commissioner may extend 6-week consultation period by 1 month for complex processing
Commissioner must inform controller of any extension before end of 1-month period

sections.source_quote: The controller must consult the Commissioner prior to the processing if a data protection impact assessment prepared under section 64 indicates that the processing of the data would result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals (in the absence of measures to mitigate the risk).

sections.heading: Security of processing
sections.title: Security measures for personal data processing
sections.summary: Controllers and processors must implement appropriate technical and organizational security measures to protect personal data based on risk assessment. Automated processing requires additional risk-based measures. Adherence to approved codes of conduct may demonstrate compliance with security requirements. This applies to all data processing activities involving personal information.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires implementation of risk-appropriate security measures for data processing, critical for ensuring data protection in high-risk operations and compliance with regulatory expectations.

requirements.text: Controllers and processors must implement appropriate technical and organizational security measures
For automated processing, controllers and processors must implement risk-based measures
Adherence to approved code of conduct may demonstrate compliance with security requirements

sections.source_quote: Each controller and each processor must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risks arising from the processing of personal data.

sections.heading: Notification of a personal data breach to the Commissioner
sections.title: Notification of personal data breaches to Commissioner
sections.summary: Controllers must notify the Information Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming aware of a personal data breach, unless the breach poses no significant risk to individuals. Notifications must include breach details, contact information, and remedial actions. Controllers must record breach facts, effects, and remedial actions for verification purposes. Processors must notify controllers without undue delay.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely breach notification to regulator with specific details, critical for assessing breach impact and implementing corrective measures in data protection frameworks.

requirements.text: Controller must notify Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming aware of personal data breach
Notification must include breach description, affected data subjects, and personal data records
Controller must provide contact details of data protection officer
Controller must record facts, effects, and remedial actions of breach
Controller must ensure breach information is recorded for Commissioner verification
Processor must notify controller without undue delay of personal data breach

sections.source_quote: If a controller becomes aware of a personal data breach in relation to personal data for which the controller is responsible, the controller must notify the breach to the Commissioner—where feasible, not later than 72 hours after becoming aware of it.

sections.heading: Communication of a personal data breach to the data subject
sections.title: Notification of personal data breaches to data subjects
sections.summary: Controllers must inform data subjects of personal data breaches without undue delay if the breach poses high risk to their rights and freedoms. Information must include breach description and contact details. Controllers may avoid notification if they have implemented sufficient security measures or subsequent actions mitigate the risk. The Commissioner may require notification if the controller has not informed the data subject.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely notification to data subjects for high-risk breaches, with conditions for exemption based on security measures and risk mitigation.

requirements.text: Controller must inform data subject of breach without undue delay if breach poses high risk to rights and freedoms
Notification must include breach description and contact details of data protection officer
Controller may avoid notification if security measures prevent breach impact
Controller may avoid notification if subsequent actions mitigate risk
Controller may avoid notification if disproportionate effort required

sections.source_quote: Where a personal data breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals, the controller must inform the data subject of the breach without undue delay.

sections.heading: Designation of a data protection officer
sections.title: Designation of data protection officer
sections.summary: Controllers must designate a data protection officer unless acting as a court or judicial authority. The officer must have expertise in data protection law and be capable of performing tasks under section 71. Controllers must publish contact details and communicate them to the Information Commissioner.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires appointment of qualified data protection officer for regulatory oversight, critical for ensuring compliance with data protection obligations.

requirements.text: Controller must designate data protection officer unless acting as court or judicial authority
Designated officer must have expertise in data protection law and practice
Designated officer must be capable of performing tasks under section 71
Controller must publish contact details of data protection officer
Controller must communicate contact details to Information Commissioner

sections.source_quote: The controller must designate a data protection officer, unless the controller is a court, or other judicial authority, acting in its judicial capacity.

sections.heading: Position of data protection officer
sections.title: Data protection officer role and responsibilities
sections.summary: Controllers must ensure their data protection officer is actively involved in all personal data protection matters. The officer requires necessary resources and access to data to perform specific tasks and maintain expertise. Controllers must prevent conflicts of interest, ensure the officer cannot be dismissed for fulfilling duties, and maintain direct reporting to highest management. Data subjects can contact the officer regarding their data processing or rights.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires a dedicated data protection officer with specific responsibilities to ensure compliance with data protection laws during cross-border transfers and processing activities.

requirements.text: Controller must ensure data protection officer is involved in all personal data protection issues
Controller must provide data protection officer with necessary resources and access to personal data
Data protection officer must maintain expert knowledge of data protection law
Controller must ensure data protection officer does not receive instructions on section 71 tasks
Controller must ensure data protection officer does not perform conflicting tasks
Controller must not dismiss data protection officer for performing section 71 tasks
Data subject may contact data protection officer regarding their data processing
Data protection officer must report to highest management level of controller

sections.source_quote: 70. (1) The controller must ensure that the data protection officer is involved, properly and in a timely manner, in all issues which relate to the protection of personal data.

sections.heading: Tasks of data protection officer
sections.title: Data protection officer tasks and responsibilities
sections.summary: Controllers must entrust the data protection officer with specific tasks including informing about obligations, conducting data protection impact assessments, cooperating with the commissioner, and maintaining expertise. The officer must report directly to the controller's highest management and handle data subject inquiries regarding their rights.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines specific tasks for data protection officers to ensure compliance with data protection laws during cross-border transfers and processing activities.

requirements.text: Controller must entrust data protection officer with specific tasks including informing about obligations
Data protection officer must conduct data protection impact assessments
Data protection officer must cooperate with the commissioner
Data protection officer must maintain expertise in data protection law
Data protection officer must report to highest management level of controller
Data subject may contact data protection officer regarding their data processing

sections.source_quote: 71. (1) The controller must ensure that the data protection officer is involved, properly and in a timely manner, in all issues which relate to the protection of personal data.

sections.title: Data protection test for cross-border transfers
sections.summary: The data protection test determines whether transfers to third countries or international organizations meet equivalent data protection standards as under this Act. The Secretary of State must consider factors including rule of law, human rights, enforcement mechanisms, judicial redress, international obligations, and cultural context when evaluating the test.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes the criteria for determining whether cross-border data transfers to third countries meet equivalent data protection standards as required by the UK.

requirements.text: Data protection test is met if standard of protection for law enforcement processing in country is not materially lower than standards under this Act

sections.title: Cross-border data transfers requiring appropriate safeguards
sections.summary: A transfer of personal data to a third country or international organisation is permitted only if either an appropriate legal instrument binds the recipient or the controller reasonably considers the data protection test is met. The controller must document transfers relying on this provision and provide documentation to the Commissioner upon request. Documentation must include transfer date/time, recipient details, justification, and data description.
sections.dtia_summary: Transfers to third countries require documented safeguards either through binding legal instruments or controller assessment of data protection standards. This section mandates specific documentation for transfers to ensure compliance with data protection requirements.

sections.title: Certification of national security exemptions for data processing
sections.summary: This section establishes a certification process for national security exemptions from data processing restrictions. A Minister of the Crown must issue a certificate confirming exemption from specific data processing provisions when safeguarding national security. The certificate is conclusive evidence of exemption, and affected parties may appeal to the Tribunal if they believe the certificate is invalid. The process includes specific conditions for certification and appeal mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates a mechanism for national security exemptions that could impact data transfers when national security justifies bypassing standard data protection rules. The certification process requires specific safeguards to ensure legitimate national security interests are protected without compromising data privacy.

sections.title: Data transmission to non-UK recipients with national security restrictions
sections.summary: This section requires controllers to ensure that personal data transmitted to non-UK recipients (third countries or international organizations) for law enforcement purposes complies with UK data protection restrictions. The controller must inform the recipient that the data is subject to the same restrictions as if it had been processed within the UK, and the recipient must comply with those restrictions. This applies specifically to intelligence services and their designated competent authorities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates that cross-border transfers for national security purposes must maintain equivalent data protection restrictions as domestic processing, requiring explicit compliance with UK data rules by foreign recipients.

sections.title: Fourth data protection principle: accuracy and timeliness
sections.summary: This section states that personal data must be accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date. It establishes the fourth data protection principle, emphasizing the need for data accuracy and timely updates to ensure data reliability and relevance for processing purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires data to be accurate and current, which impacts data transfer and storage practices to ensure compliance with data quality standards across borders.

sections.title: Prohibition on automated decisions affecting individuals
sections.summary: Controllers cannot make decisions significantly affecting data subjects based solely on automated processing of personal data. Exceptions include legally required decisions, explicit consent, and decisions related to contractual steps. A decision significantly affecting an individual is one with legal effects on them. Automated processing requires no human intervention in the decision.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts cross-border transfers of data used in automated decisions without human oversight; requires human review for high-impact decisions.

sections.source_quote: 96(1) The controller may not take a decision significantly affecting a data subject that is based on entirely automated processing of personal data relating to the data subject.

sections.heading: Right to intervene in automated decision-making
sections.title: Right to challenge automated decisions
sections.summary: Data subjects can challenge automated decisions that significantly affect them if legally required. Controllers must notify the data subject within a reasonable time and allow a 1-month window to request reconsideration or a non-automated decision. The controller must respond to such requests within 1 month.
