Specific obligations for security processing
Specific obligations for personal data breaches

sections.source_quote: 101 This Chapter sets out the general obligations of controllers and processors (see sections 102 to 106); specific obligations of controllers and processors with respect to security (see section 107); specific obligations of controllers and processors with respect to personal data breaches (see section 108).

sections.heading: General obligations of the controller
sections.title: Controller must ensure data processing compliance
sections.summary: Controllers must implement measures to ensure and demonstrate compliance with this Part's requirements to the Commissioner. This obligation applies to all data processing activities conducted by controllers. The Commissioner refers to the data protection regulator in the UK.
sections.dtia_summary: Controllers must verify their data processing complies with UK data laws before conducting transfers or processing activities. This affects cross-border data transfers by requiring compliance demonstrations.

requirements.text: Implement appropriate measures to ensure processing of personal data complies with this Part
Be able to demonstrate compliance with this Part to the Commissioner

sections.source_quote: Each controller must implement appropriate measures— (a) to ensure, and (b) to be able to demonstrate, in particular to the Commissioner, that the processing of personal data complies with the requirements of this Part.

sections.heading: Data protection by design
sections.title: Data protection by design requires risk assessment
sections.summary: Controllers must assess the impact of proposed data processing on data subjects' rights before implementation. They must implement technical and organisational measures to ensure data protection principles are followed and risks to data subjects are minimised. This applies to all processing activities initiated by controllers.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires controllers to assess data processing risks before implementation and implement safeguards to protect data subjects. Critical for cross-border transfers as it mandates risk assessments and security measures.

requirements.text: Consider impact of proposed processing on data subjects' rights and freedoms before implementation
Implement technical and organisational measures to ensure data protection principles are implemented
Implement technical and organisational measures to minimise risks to data subjects' rights and freedoms

sections.source_quote: (1) Where a controller proposes that a particular type of processing of personal data be carried out by or on behalf of the controller, the controller must, prior to the processing, consider the impact of the proposed processing on the rights and freedoms of data subjects. (2) A controller must implement appropriate technical and organisational measures which are designed to ensure that— (a) the data protection principles are implemented, and (b) risks to the rights and freedoms of data subjects are minimised.

sections.heading: Joint controllers
sections.title: Joint controllers must share compliance responsibilities
sections.summary: Joint controllers must establish transparent arrangements to define their compliance responsibilities and designate a contact point for data subjects. This applies when two or more entities jointly determine data processing purposes and means. The arrangements must not override existing legal determinations.
sections.dtia_summary: Joint controllers must coordinate compliance responsibilities and designate a data subject contact point. Critical for cross-border data transfers involving multiple entities.

requirements.text: Determine respective compliance responsibilities with transparent arrangements
Designate a controller as the contact point for data subjects

sections.source_quote: Where two or more [F352controllers] jointly determine the purposes and means of processing personal data, they are joint controllers for the purposes of this Part. Joint controllers must, in a transparent manner, determine their respective responsibilities for compliance with this Part by means of an arrangement between them, except to the extent that those responsibilities are determined under or by virtue of an enactment.

sections.heading: Processors
sections.title: Processors must comply with controller instructions
sections.summary: Controllers may use processors who implement sufficient security measures and provide information to demonstrate compliance. Processors acting in breach of this Part become controllers for that processing activity. This applies to all processing activities conducted by processors on behalf of controllers.
sections.dtia_summary: Processors must ensure compliance with data protection laws and can become controllers if they breach regulations. Important for cross-border transfers as processors often handle data internationally.

requirements.text: Implement appropriate measures to secure processing compliance with this Part
Provide information necessary for demonstrating processing compliance with this Part
Be treated as a controller if they determine processing purposes and means in breach of this Part

sections.source_quote: The controller may use only a processor who undertakes— (a) to implement appropriate measures that are sufficient to secure that the processing complies with this Part; (b) to provide to the controller such information as is necessary for demonstrating that the processing complies with this Part.

sections.heading: Processing under the authority of the controller or processor
sections.title: Processors must follow controller instructions
sections.summary: Processors and persons acting under a controller or processor's authority may not process personal data except on the controller's instructions or to comply with a legal obligation. This applies to all entities with access to personal data.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts data processing to controller instructions or legal obligations, critical for cross-border transfers to prevent unauthorized data handling.

requirements.text: May not process personal data except on instructions from the controller
May not process personal data except to comply with a legal obligation

sections.source_quote: A processor, and any person acting under the authority of a controller or processor, who has access to personal data may not process the data except— (a) on instructions from the controller, or (b) to comply with a legal obligation.

sections.heading: Security of processing
sections.title: Security measures for data processing
sections.summary: Controllers and processors must implement security measures appropriate to the risks of processing personal data. Automated processing requires additional risk-based measures to protect data systems and ensure data integrity.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates risk-based security measures for data processing, critical for cross-border transfers to protect data from breaches.

requirements.text: Implement security measures appropriate to the risks of processing personal data

sections.source_quote: Each controller and each processor must implement security measures appropriate to the risks arising from the processing of personal data.

sections.heading: Communication of a personal data breach
sections.title: Notification of serious personal data breaches to the Commissioner
sections.summary: Controllers must notify the Information Commissioner without undue delay if they become aware of a serious personal data breach involving personal data they are responsible for. If notification is delayed beyond 72 hours, the controller must provide reasons for the delay. A breach is considered serious if it seriously interferes with the rights and freedoms of data subjects. This applies to both controllers and processors, with processors required to notify the controller without undue delay.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires timely breach notification to the Commissioner for serious breaches, impacting DTIA by mandating specific breach details and delay justification for transfers outside the UK.

requirements.text: Controller must notify Commissioner without undue delay of serious personal data breach
Notification must include breach description, data subjects affected, and contact details
If notification delayed beyond 72 hours, must provide reasons for delay
Breach is serious if it seriously interferes with data subject rights
Processor must notify controller without undue delay of personal data breach

sections.source_quote: 108 (1) If a controller becomes aware of a serious personal data breach in relation to personal data for which the controller is responsible, the controller must notify the Commissioner of the breach without undue delay.

sections.heading: Transfers of personal data outside the United Kingdom
sections.title: Prohibited transfers of personal data outside the UK
sections.summary: Controllers cannot transfer personal data to countries or territories outside the UK or international organizations unless the transfer is necessary and proportionate for statutory functions or specific purposes under the Security Service Act 1989 or Intelligence Services Act 1994. This section defines the legal conditions for permissible cross-border data transfers.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets strict conditions for cross-border data transfers, requiring necessity and proportionality for specific statutory purposes, directly impacting DTIA for international data transfers.

requirements.text: Controller may not transfer personal data to countries or territories outside the UK
Controller may not transfer personal data to international organizations
Transfer must be necessary and proportionate for statutory functions
Transfer must be for purposes under Security Service Act 1989 or Intelligence Services Act 1994

sections.source_quote: 109 (1) A controller may not transfer personal data to—(a) a country or territory outside the United Kingdom, or (b) an international organisation, unless the transfer falls within subsection (2).

sections.heading: National security
sections.title: National security exemptions for data protection
sections.summary: National security can exempt personal data from certain data protection provisions, including data protection principles, data subject rights, breach notification, and specific functions of the Commissioner. A certificate signed by a Minister of the Crown certifies exemption for national security purposes, which is conclusive evidence unless appealed by affected parties.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows national security exemptions for data protection provisions, requiring certificates from Ministers for cross-border data transfers and other operations, directly impacting DTIA for security-sensitive transfers.

requirements.text: Exemption from data protection principles applies for national security
Exemption from data subject rights applies for national security
Exemption from breach notification applies for national security
Certificate signed by Minister of Crown certifies exemption for national security
Affected person may appeal certificate to Tribunal

sections.source_quote: 110 (1) A provision mentioned in subsection (2) does not apply to personal data to which this Part applies if exemption from the provision is required for the purpose of safeguarding national security.

sections.heading: Power to make further exemptions
sections.title: Power to make further exemptions
sections.summary: The Secretary of State may issue regulations to add or remove exemptions from data protection provisions. These regulations require affirmative resolution procedure and apply to Schedule 11, which contains exemptions for national security purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for additional exemptions through regulations, impacting DTIA by enabling further national security exemptions for data transfers and processing.

requirements.text: Secretary of State may add exemptions from data protection provisions
Secretary of State may remove exemptions added by regulations
Regulations require affirmative resolution procedure

sections.source_quote: 113 (1) The Secretary of State may by regulations amend Schedule 11—(a) by adding exemptions from any provision of this Part;

sections.heading: The Information Commissioner
sections.title: Information Commissioner role
sections.summary: The Information Commissioner is established as a body corporate with specific functions defined in Schedule 12. This section ensures the Commissioner's continued role in data protection oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines the role of the Information Commissioner, impacting DTIA by establishing oversight for data transfers and breaches.

requirements.text: Information Commissioner is established as a body corporate

sections.source_quote: 114 (1) There is to continue to be an Information Commissioner.

sections.heading: The Information Commission
sections.title: Information Commission role
sections.summary: The Information Commission is established as a body corporate with further provisions defined in Schedule 12A. This section replaces the Information Commissioner with the Commission for data protection oversight.
sections.dtia_summary: Replaces the Information Commissioner with the Information Commission, impacting DTIA by establishing new oversight mechanisms for data transfers and breaches.

requirements.text: Information Commission is established as a body corporate

sections.source_quote: 114A (1) A body corporate called the Information Commission is established.

sections.heading: General functions under the [F357UK GDPR] and safeguards
sections.title: Commissioner's duties and powers under GDPR
sections.summary: The Commissioner is granted specific functions under GDPR articles 57 and 58, including advising Parliament on data protection measures and issuing opinions on data protection issues. These functions are subject to safeguards outlined in subsections (5) to (9). The Commissioner's powers to require information, conduct audits, and access premises are restricted to specific procedures and notices.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the Commissioner's oversight powers that require specific procedural safeguards for data protection activities, impacting how data transfers and processing are monitored across borders.

requirements.text: The Commissioner must advise Parliament, government, and institutions on legislative and administrative measures for data protection rights and freedoms
The Commissioner may issue opinions on data protection issues to Parliament, government, institutions, and the public
The Commissioner's power to require information is exercisable only by giving an information notice under section 142
The Commissioner's power to conduct data protection audits is exercisable only in accordance with section 146
The Commissioner's powers to obtain information and access premises are exercisable only in accordance with Schedule 15 or section 146
The Commissioner's corrective powers may be exercised only by giving an enforcement notice under section 149
The Commissioner's power to order certification bodies to withdraw or not issue certifications is exercisable only by giving an enforcement notice under section 149
The Commissioner's administrative fines powers are exercisable only by giving a penalty notice under section 155

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner's functions in relation to the processing of personal data to which the [F362UK GDPR] applies include—(a) a duty to advise Parliament, the government and other institutions and bodies on legislative and administrative measures relating to the protection of individuals' rights and freedoms with regard to the processing of personal data, and (b) a power to issue, on the Commissioner's own initiative or on request, opinions to Parliament, the government or other institutions and bodies as well as to the public on any issue related to the protection of personal data.

sections.heading: Other general functions
sections.title: Commissioner's monitoring responsibilities for data protection
sections.summary: The Commissioner is responsible for monitoring the application of data protection laws to protect fundamental rights and freedoms, particularly in law enforcement contexts. This includes being the designated authority for Article 13 of the Data Protection Convention and overseeing processing activities outside GDPR scope.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the Commissioner's role in monitoring data processing activities, which affects how data transfers and processing are assessed for compliance with international obligations.

requirements.text: The Commissioner is responsible for monitoring the application of Part 3 of this Act to protect fundamental rights and freedoms in law enforcement data processing
The Commissioner remains the designated authority for Article 13 of the Data Protection Convention
Schedule 13 confers additional general functions on the Commissioner for processing outside GDPR scope

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner is responsible for monitoring the application of Part 3 of this Act, in order to protect the fundamental rights and freedoms of individuals in relation to processing by a competent authority for any of the law enforcement purposes (as defined in Part 3) and to facilitate the free flow of personal data.

sections.heading: Competence in relation to courts etc
sections.title: Commissioner's non-competence in judicial data processing
sections.summary: This section explicitly states that the Commissioner cannot exercise functions related to personal data processing by individuals or courts acting in judicial capacity. This limitation applies to both the UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section clarifies that the Commissioner does not have authority over judicial data processing activities, which affects how data transfers involving judicial entities are handled.

requirements.text: The Commissioner cannot exercise functions in relation to personal data processing by individuals acting in judicial capacity
The Commissioner cannot exercise functions in relation to personal data processing by courts or tribunals acting in judicial capacity

sections.source_quote: Nothing in this Act [F374or the UK GDPR] permits or requires the Commissioner to exercise functions in relation to the processing of personal data by—(a) an individual acting in a judicial capacity, or (b) a court or tribunal acting in its judicial capacity

sections.heading: [F376Co-operation between parties to the Data Protection Convention]
sections.title: Cooperation mechanisms for data protection conventions
sections.summary: This section outlines cooperation mechanisms between parties to the Data Protection Convention, though it has been largely repealed as of 2020. It specifies that Part 2 of Schedule 14 details the Commissioner's functions for cooperation under Article 13 of the Data Protection Convention.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes cooperation mechanisms for international data protection, which may impact cross-border data transfers and international data sharing arrangements.

requirements.text: Part 2 of Schedule 14 makes provision for the Commissioner's functions under Article 13 of the Data Protection Convention

sections.source_quote: Part 2 of Schedule 14 makes provision for the Commissioner's functions under Article 13 of the Data Protection Convention

sections.heading: Inspection of personal data in accordance with international obligations
sections.title: Inspection of personal data for international obligations
sections.summary: The Commissioner may inspect personal data when necessary to fulfill international obligations, subject to specific restrictions. This includes inspecting automated systems, filing systems, and equipment used for data processing. The Commissioner must provide written notice before inspection, with exceptions for urgent cases.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides the Commissioner's authority to inspect personal data for international obligations, which affects how data transfers are monitored and assessed for compliance.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may inspect personal data where necessary to discharge international obligations
The Commissioner may inspect personal data processed by automated means or filing systems
The Commissioner must provide written notice to controllers and processors before inspecting personal data
The Commissioner may inspect equipment used for data processing
The Commissioner does not need to provide written notice if the case is urgent

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner may inspect personal data where the inspection is necessary in order to discharge an international obligation of the United Kingdom, subject to the restriction in subsection (2).

sections.heading: Further international role
sections.title: International co-operation for data protection
sections.summary: The Commissioner must take steps to develop international cooperation mechanisms for data protection enforcement, provide mutual assistance in data protection enforcement, engage stakeholders in international discussions, and promote the exchange of legislation and practices. This applies to data processing outside GDPR scope, with specific duties for international organizations and British overseas territories.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the Commissioner's international co-operation responsibilities, which directly impact how data transfers and processing are assessed for compliance with international obligations.

requirements.text: The Commissioner must develop international cooperation mechanisms for effective enforcement of data protection legislation
The Commissioner must provide international mutual assistance in data protection enforcement, subject to appropriate safeguards
The Commissioner must engage stakeholders in discussions to further international cooperation in data protection enforcement
The Commissioner must promote the exchange and documentation of legislation and practices for data protection
The Commissioner may contribute to activities of international organizations with data protection functions
The Commissioner must carry out data protection functions directed by the Secretary of State for international obligations
The Commissioner may provide assistance to authorities in British overseas territories

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner must, in relation to third countries and international organisations, take appropriate steps to—(a) develop international co-operation mechanisms to facilitate the effective enforcement of legislation for the protection of personal data; (b) provide international mutual assistance in the enforcement of legislation for the protection of personal data, subject to appropriate safeguards for the protection of personal data and fundamental rights and freedoms; (c) engage relevant stakeholders in discussion and activities aimed at furthering international co-operation in the enforcement of legislation for the protection of personal data; (d) promote the exchange and documentation of legislation and practice for the protection of personal data, including legislation and practice relating to jurisdictional conflicts with third countries.

sections.heading: Principal objective
sections.title: Principal objective of data protection commissioner
sections.summary: The Commissioner must prioritize securing adequate personal data protection while balancing data subjects' interests, controller needs, and public interest considerations. The Commissioner must also promote public trust and confidence in personal data processing activities. This objective applies to the Commissioner's regulatory functions under data protection legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the Commissioner's overarching goals for data protection, emphasizing the need to balance data subject rights with public interest and trust in data processing activities. It sets the foundation for how the Commissioner approaches data transfer and processing decisions.

requirements.text: The Commissioner must secure an appropriate level of protection for personal data
The Commissioner must consider data subjects' interests, controllers' interests, and matters of public interest
The Commissioner must promote public trust and confidence in personal data processing

sections.source_quote: It is the principal objective of the Commissioner, in carrying out functions under the data protection legislation—(a) to secure an appropriate level of protection for personal data, having regard to the interests of data subjects, controllers and others and matters of general public interest, and (b) to promote public trust and confidence in the processing of personal data.

sections.heading: Duties in relation to functions under the data protection legislation
sections.title: Duties for data protection commissioner functions
sections.summary: The Commissioner must consider factors such as promoting innovation, competition, criminal investigations, national security, and children's specific data protection needs when exercising data protection functions. These considerations apply to the Commissioner's regulatory decisions and strategies under data protection legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines key considerations the Commissioner must balance when making decisions about data transfers and processing, particularly regarding children's data and national security implications.

requirements.text: The Commissioner must consider promoting innovation when exercising data protection functions
The Commissioner must consider promoting competition when exercising data protection functions
The Commissioner must consider preventing, investigating, detecting and prosecuting criminal offences
The Commissioner must consider safeguarding public security and national security
The Commissioner must consider children's specific data protection needs

sections.source_quote: In carrying out functions under the data protection legislation, the Commissioner must have regard to such of the following as appear to the Commissioner to be relevant in the circumstances—(a) the desirability of promoting innovation; (b) the desirability of promoting competition; (c) the importance of the prevention, investigation, detection and prosecution of criminal offences; (d) the need to safeguard public security and national security; (e) the fact that children merit specific protection with regard to their personal data because they may be less aware of the risks and consequences associated with processing of personal data and of their rights in relation to such processing.

sections.heading: Strategy
sections.title: Commissioner's strategy for data protection functions
sections.summary: The Commissioner must develop a strategy aligned with their duties under sections 120A, 120B, and specific regulatory frameworks. The strategy must be reviewed periodically and revised as needed, and must be published to ensure transparency and accountability in data protection practices.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires the Commissioner to create a strategic framework for data protection that addresses innovation, competition, and public interest, which impacts how data transfers and processing are evaluated for compliance.

requirements.text: The Commissioner must prepare a strategy for carrying out functions under data protection legislation
The strategy must align with the Commissioner's duties under sections 120A and 120B
The strategy must align with section 108 of the Deregulation Act 2015
The strategy must align with section 21 of the Legislative and Regulatory Reform Act 2006
The Commissioner must review the strategy from time to time
The Commissioner must revise the strategy as appropriate
The Commissioner must publish the strategy and any revised strategy

sections.source_quote: (1) The Commissioner must prepare a strategy for carrying out the Commissioner's functions under the data protection legislation in accordance with the Commissioner's duties under—(a) sections 120A and 120B, (b) section 108 of the Deregulation Act 2015 (exercise of regulatory functions: economic growth), and (c) section 21 of the Legislative and Regulatory Reform Act 2006 (exercise of regulatory functions: principles). (2) The Commissioner must—(a) review the strategy from time to time, and (b) revise the strategy as appropriate. (3) The Commissioner must publish the strategy and any revised strategy.

sections.heading: Duty to consult other regulators
sections.title: Duty to consult other regulators
sections.summary: The Commissioner must consult relevant regulators when data protection functions could impact economic growth, innovation, or competition. This consultation is required to ensure alignment with other regulatory frameworks and to address potential conflicts in data protection approaches.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates coordination with other regulators when data transfers or processing activities might affect economic growth or competition, which is critical for cross-border data transfers involving multiple jurisdictions.

requirements.text: The Commissioner must consult other regulators about how data protection functions affect economic growth, innovation and competition
The persons to consult include those exercising regulatory functions as the Commissioner considers appropriate

sections.source_quote: (1) The Commissioner must, at such times as the Commissioner considers appropriate, consult the persons mentioned in subsection (2) about how the manner in which the Commissioner exercises functions under the data protection legislation may affect economic growth, innovation and competition. (2) The persons are—(a) such persons exercising regulatory functions as the Commissioner considers appropriate;

sections.heading: Data-sharing code
sections.title: Data-sharing code of practice
sections.summary: This section defines the Commissioner's role in providing practical guidance and good practice for sharing personal data while ensuring compliance with data protection legislation. It specifies that 'good practice' must consider data subjects' interests and adherence to data protection requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes standards for data sharing that must be considered when transferring data across borders, ensuring that sharing practices align with data protection principles and minimize risks.

requirements.text: The Commissioner must provide practical guidance on sharing personal data in accordance with data protection legislation
The Commissioner must define 'good practice in the sharing of personal data' as practices that appear desirable considering data subjects' interests and compliance with data protection legislation
'The sharing of personal data' means disclosure of personal data by transmission, dissemination or making it available

sections.source_quote: 121 Data-sharing code (a) practical guidance in relation to the sharing of personal data in accordance with the requirements of the data protection legislation, and (b) good practice in the sharing of personal data. 'good practice in the sharing of personal data' means such practice in the sharing of personal data as appears to the Commissioner to be desirable having regard to the interests of data subjects and others, including compliance with the requirements of the data protection legislation; 'the sharing of personal data' means the disclosure of personal data by transmission, dissemination or otherwise making it available;

sections.heading: Direct marketing code
sections.title: Direct marketing code of practice
sections.summary: This section defines the Commissioner's role in providing practical guidance and good practice for direct marketing activities, ensuring compliance with data protection legislation and the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations. It specifies that 'direct marketing' must consider data subjects' interests and legal compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes standards for direct marketing that must be considered when transferring data for marketing purposes, ensuring that practices align with data protection principles and minimize risks.

requirements.text: The Commissioner must provide practical guidance on direct marketing in accordance with data protection legislation and the Privacy and Electronic Communications Regulations
The Commissioner must define 'direct marketing' as communication of advertising or marketing material directed to particular individuals
The Commissioner must define 'good practice in direct marketing' as practices that appear desirable considering data subjects' interests and compliance with data protection legislation

sections.source_quote: 122 Direct marketing code (a) practical guidance in relation to the carrying out of direct marketing in accordance with the requirements of the data protection legislation and the Privacy and Electronic Communications (EC Directive) Regulations 2003 (S.I. 2003/2426), and (b) good practice in direct marketing. 'direct marketing' means the communication (by whatever means) of advertising or marketing material which is directed to particular individuals; 'good practice in direct marketing' means such practice in direct marketing as appears to the Commissioner to be desirable having regard to the interests of data subjects and others, including compliance with the requirements mentioned in subsection (1)(a);

sections.heading: Age-appropriate design code
sections.title: Age-appropriate design for children's digital services
sections.summary: The Commissioner must create a code of practice for age-appropriate design of information society services accessed by children. The code must consult children, parents, child development experts, and trade associations. The code must consider age-related needs and UK obligations under the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child. Transitional provisions must cease after 12 months of implementation.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires codes for age-appropriate design of children's digital services with specific consultation requirements and UN Convention alignment. Affects data transfer assessments for services targeting children.

requirements.text: Commissioner must prepare a code of practice on age-appropriate design of information society services for children
Commissioner must consult children, parents, child development experts, and trade associations
Code must consider age-related needs of children
Code must align with UK obligations under UN Convention on the Rights of the Child
Transitional provisions in first code must cease after 12 months

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner must prepare a code of practice which contains such guidance as the Commissioner considers appropriate on standards of age-appropriate design of relevant information society services which are likely to be accessed by children.

sections.heading: Data protection and journalism code
sections.title: Journalism data processing best practices
sections.summary: The Commissioner must develop practical guidance for journalism-related data processing that complies with data protection laws. The guidance must consider data subject interests and public interest in free expression. It includes compliance with data protection legislation and may have transitional provisions. The Commissioner must consult data subjects and other relevant parties.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides standards for journalism data processing that may impact cross-border data transfers involving media organizations. Requires alignment with data protection principles for journalistic activities.

requirements.text: Commissioner must prepare practical guidance for journalism data processing
Guidance must consider data subject interests and public interest in free expression
Guidance must comply with data protection legislation
Commissioner must consult data subjects and other relevant parties

sections.source_quote: practical guidance in relation to the processing of personal data for the purposes of journalism in accordance with the requirements of the data protection legislation

sections.heading: Impact assessments for codes of practice
sections.title: Impact assessments for codes of practice
sections.summary: The Commissioner must assess and publish who would be affected by codes of practice and the likely effects on them. This applies to both initial codes and amendments. The assessment focuses on identifying affected parties and potential impacts.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires impact assessments for codes of practice to identify affected parties and potential impacts. Critical for data transfer assessments involving children or journalism.

requirements.text: Commissioner must assess who would be affected by codes of practice
Commissioner must assess the likely effects on affected parties

sections.source_quote: Where a code is prepared under section 121, 122, 123, 124 or 124A, the Commissioner must carry out and publish an assessment of who would be likely to be affected by the code, and the effect the code would be likely to have on them.

sections.heading: Approval of codes prepared under sections 121 to [F392124A]
sections.title: Code approval process
sections.summary: The Commissioner must submit codes to Parliament for approval within 40 days. If Parliament rejects a code, the Commissioner cannot issue it. If approved, the code takes effect after 21 days. The Commissioner may prepare alternative versions if rejected.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires parliamentary approval for codes of practice, affecting data transfer assessments by introducing a formal review mechanism before implementation.

requirements.text: Commissioner must submit codes to Parliament for approval
Parliament has 40 days to approve codes
If rejected, Commissioner cannot issue code
Approved codes take effect after 21 days
Commissioner may prepare alternative versions if rejected

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner must submit a code to Parliament for approval within 40 days.

sections.heading: Publication and review of codes issued under section 125(4)
sections.title: Code publication and review
sections.summary: The Commissioner must publish codes issued under section 125(4). The Commissioner must keep codes under review and may use powers to remedy breaches of international obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires ongoing review of codes to ensure compliance with international obligations, critical for data transfer assessments involving cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: Commissioner must publish codes issued under section 125(4)
Commissioner must keep codes under review
Commissioner may exercise powers to remedy breaches of international obligations

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner must publish a code issued under section 125(4).

sections.heading: Effect of codes issued under section 125(4)
sections.title: Code admissibility in legal proceedings
sections.summary: Failure to comply with codes does not constitute legal liability. Codes are admissible in evidence and courts must consider them when relevant to proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: Codes of practice are admissible in legal proceedings, affecting data transfer assessments by providing legal standards for compliance.

requirements.text: Failure to comply with codes does not constitute legal liability
Codes are admissible in legal proceedings
Courts must consider codes when relevant to proceedings

sections.source_quote: A failure by a person to act in accordance with a provision of a code issued under section 125(4) does not of itself make that person liable to legal proceedings in a court or tribunal.

sections.heading: Consensual audits
sections.title: Consensual audits of data processing compliance
sections.summary: The Commissioner may assess a controller or processor's compliance with good practice in personal data processing with their consent. The Commissioner must inform the controller or processor of the assessment results. 'Good practice' is defined as in section 124A.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows regulators to audit data processing practices with consent; relevant for assessing compliance with data handling standards in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may assess a controller or processor's compliance with good practice in personal data processing with their consent
The Commissioner must inform the controller or processor of the assessment results

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner's functions under Article 58(1) of the GDPR and paragraph 1 of Schedule 13 include power, with the consent of a controller or processor, to carry out an assessment of whether the controller or processor is complying with good practice in the processing of personal data.

sections.heading: Records of national security certificates
sections.title: Public records of national security certificates
sections.summary: Ministers issuing national security certificates must send copies to the Commissioner. The Commissioner must publish records containing the issuer's name, issue date, and certificate text (subject to national security restrictions). The Commissioner must keep these records publicly accessible while the certificate is valid and notify the Commissioner of revocations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires public disclosure of national security certificates with safeguards against sensitive information; relevant for data sharing with third parties in security contexts.

requirements.text: A Minister of the Crown who issues a certificate under section 27, 79 or 111 must send a copy of the certificate to the Commissioner
The Commissioner must publish a record of the certificate containing the name of the Minister who issued the certificate
The Commissioner must publish a record of the certificate containing the date on which the certificate was issued
The Commissioner must publish a record of the certificate containing the text of the certificate (subject to national security restrictions)
The Commissioner must not publish the text of the certificate if the Minister determines it would be against national security interests
The Commissioner must not publish the text of the certificate if the Minister determines it would be contrary to public interest
The Commissioner must not publish the text of the certificate if the Minister determines it might jeopardise the safety of any person
The Commissioner must keep the record of the certificate available to the public while the certificate is in force
If a Minister revokes a certificate, the Minister must notify the Commissioner

sections.source_quote: A Minister of the Crown who issues a certificate under section 27, 79 or 111 must send a copy of the certificate to the Commissioner.

sections.heading: Disclosure of information to the Commissioner
sections.title: Information disclosure to Data Protection Commissioner
sections.summary: Individuals may provide information to the Commissioner necessary for their functions, except where prohibited by law. This section does not override prohibitions under the Investigatory Powers Act 2016. The provision remains effective until the repeal of Part 1 of the Regulation of Investigatory Powers Act 2000.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows data subjects to disclose information to regulators without legal barriers; relevant for data sharing with authorities in compliance contexts.

requirements.text: No enactment or rule of law prohibiting or restricting the disclosure of information precludes a person from providing the Commissioner with information necessary for the discharge of the Commissioner's functions
This section does not authorise the making of a disclosure which is prohibited by any of Parts 1 to 7 or Chapter 1 of Part 9 of the Investigatory Powers Act 2016

sections.source_quote: No enactment or rule of law prohibiting or restricting the disclosure of information precludes a person from providing the Commissioner with information necessary for the discharge of the Commissioner's functions.

sections.heading: Confidentiality of information
sections.title: Confidentiality of information obtained by Commissioner
sections.summary: Commissioner staff must not disclose information obtained for their functions unless authorized. Disclosure requires consent from the individual or business, the information must be relevant to an individual or business, and not already public. Disclosure is lawful for public interest, criminal/civil proceedings, or legitimate interests.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets strict confidentiality rules for data obtained by regulators; critical for ensuring data protection in cross-border data transfers involving sensitive information.

requirements.text: A person who is or has been the Commissioner, or a member of the Commissioner's staff or an agent of the Commissioner must not disclose information which relates to an identified or identifiable individual or business
A person must not disclose information which is not available to the public from other sources at the time of disclosure and has not previously been available to the public from other sources
A disclosure is made with lawful authority only if the disclosure was made with the consent of the individual or the person carrying on the business
A disclosure is made with lawful authority only if the information was obtained for the purpose of being made available to the public
A disclosure is made with lawful authority only if the disclosure is necessary for the discharge of the Commissioner's functions
A disclosure is made with lawful authority only if the disclosure is for the purposes of criminal or civil proceedings
A disclosure is made with lawful authority only if the disclosure is necessary in the public interest

sections.source_quote: A person who is or has been the Commissioner, or a member of the Commissioner's staff or an agent of the Commissioner, must not disclose information which—(a) has been obtained by, or provided to, the Commissioner in the course of, or for the purposes of, the discharging of the Commissioner's functions, (b) relates to an identified or identifiable individual or business, and (c) is not available to the public from other sources at the time of the disclosure and has not previously been available to the public from other sources, unless the disclosure is made with lawful authority.

sections.heading: Guidance about privileged communications
sections.title: Guidance on privileged communications
sections.summary: The Commissioner must publish guidance on securing privileged communications for their functions and complying with legal restrictions. The Commissioner must consult the Secretary of State before publishing guidance and lay it before Parliament. Privileged communications include legal advice and communications related to legal proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires specific guidance on handling privileged communications; relevant for data sharing with third parties in legal contexts.

requirements.text: The Commissioner must produce and publish guidance about how the Commissioner proposes to secure that privileged communications are used or disclosed only so far as necessary for carrying out those functions
The Commissioner must produce and publish guidance about how the Commissioner proposes to comply with restrictions and prohibitions on obtaining or having access to privileged communications
The Commissioner must consult the Secretary of State before publishing guidance under this section
The Commissioner must arrange for guidance under this section to be laid before Parliament

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner must produce and publish guidance about—(a) how the Commissioner proposes to secure that privileged communications which the Commissioner obtains or has access to in the course of carrying out the Commissioner's functions are used or disclosed only so far as necessary for carrying out those functions, and (b) how the Commissioner proposes to comply with restrictions and prohibitions on obtaining or having access to privileged communications which are imposed by an enactment.

sections.heading: Fees for services
sections.title: Fees for data protection services
sections.summary: The Commissioner may require reasonable fees from non-data subjects or data protection officers for services provided under data protection legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for fee collection from entities for data protection services; relevant for financial compliance in data handling.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may require a person other than a data subject or a data protection officer to pay a reasonable fee for a service provided to the person, or at the person's request, which the Commissioner is required or authorised to provide under the data protection legislation

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner may require a person other than a data subject or a data protection officer to pay a reasonable fee for a service provided to the person, or at the person's request, which the Commissioner is required or authorised to provide under the data protection legislation.

sections.heading: Manifestly unfounded or excessive requests by data subjects etc
sections.title: Handling manifestly unfounded or excessive data subject requests
sections.summary: This section allows the Commissioner to charge reasonable fees or refuse action on data subject requests that are manifestly unfounded or excessive. It specifies that requests repeating previous requests may be considered excessive. The Commissioner bears the burden of proving such requests are manifestly unfounded or excessive in legal proceedings. Section 134's fee provisions apply in cases where fees are charged.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by establishing conditions under which data subject requests can be refused or charged fees, relevant when assessing the feasibility of handling cross-border data requests.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may charge a reasonable fee for dealing with the request
The Commissioner may refuse to act on the request
An example of an excessive request is one that repeats the substance of previous requests
In proceedings regarding manifestly unfounded or excessive requests, the Commissioner must show it is so

sections.source_quote: This section makes provision about cases in which a request made to the Commissioner, to which the Commissioner is required or authorised to respond under the data protection legislation, is manifestly unfounded or excessive.

sections.heading: Guidance about fees
sections.title: Commissioner's fee guidance publication requirements
sections.summary: The Commissioner must publish guidance on fees charged under sections 134 or 135, and consult the Secretary of State before publishing this guidance. This section ensures transparency in fee structures and involves government consultation to inform fee policies.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIAs by requiring the Commissioner to publish fee guidance, which may affect the cost structure for data transfers involving the Commissioner.

requirements.text: The Commissioner must produce and publish guidance about fees the Commissioner proposes to charge
Before publishing the guidance, the Commissioner must consult the Secretary of State

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner must produce and publish guidance about the fees the Commissioner proposes to charge in accordance with—section 134 or 135

sections.heading: Charges payable to the Commissioner by controllers
sections.title: Charges payable by data controllers to the Commissioner
sections.summary: The Secretary of State may require data controllers to pay charges to the Commissioner through regulations. These charges can be set for specific payment periods, discounted scenarios, refunds, or no charges. Regulations must ensure charges cover the Commissioner's expenses, including those from data protection legislation, the 1998 Act, and other relevant laws.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by establishing fee structures for data controllers, which may influence the cost of data transfers and processing activities involving the Commissioner.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State may require controllers to pay charges to the Commissioner
Regulations may require payment regardless of whether the Commissioner provides a service
Regulations may specify payment periods for charges
Regulations may provide for discounted charges
Regulations may provide for no charges in certain cases
Regulations may provide for refunds of paid charges
Regulations must cover expenses incurred by the Commissioner under data protection legislation
Regulations must cover expenses under the Data Protection Act 1998
Regulations must cover expenses under the Digital Economy Act 2017
Regulations must cover expenses under the Privacy and Electronic Communications (EC Directive) Regulations 2003
Regulations must cover expenses of the Secretary of State attributable to Commissioner functions
Regulations may require controllers to provide information to the Commissioner

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State may by regulations require controllers to pay charges of an amount specified in the regulations to the Commissioner.

sections.heading: Regulations under section 137: supplementary
sections.title: Supplementary regulations for fee charges
sections.summary: Before making fee regulations, the Secretary of State must consult affected parties and review the regulations' operation after five years. Regulations are subject to negative resolution if they only increase charges based on retail price index changes, otherwise they follow affirmative resolution procedures. These regulations do not apply to specific royal entities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIAs by detailing the regulatory process for fee adjustments, which affects the cost structure and transparency of data transfer fees.

requirements.text: Before making regulations, the Secretary of State must consult affected representatives
The Secretary of State must review regulations after five years
Regulations increasing charges based on retail price index changes are subject to negative resolution
Regulations are subject to affirmative resolution procedures unless they only increase charges based on retail price index changes
Regulations do not apply to Her Majesty in her private capacity
Regulations do not apply to Her Majesty in right of the Duchy of Lancaster
Regulations do not apply to the Duke of Cornwall

sections.source_quote: Before making regulations under section 137(1) or (6), the Secretary of State must consult such representatives of persons likely to be affected by the regulations as the Secretary of State thinks appropriate

sections.heading: Reporting to Parliament
sections.title: Annual reporting to Parliament on Commissioner's functions
sections.summary: The Commissioner must produce an annual report on their functions, lay it before Parliament, and publish it. The report must include a review of compliance with specific duties under data protection legislation, including sections 120A, 120B, 108 of the Deregulation Act 2015, and section 21 of the Legislative and Regulatory Reform Act 2006. The Commissioner may also produce additional reports.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIAs by requiring annual reporting on the Commissioner's compliance with data protection duties, which may influence the oversight of data transfers.

requirements.text: The Commissioner must produce an annual report on the carrying out of their functions
The report must be laid before Parliament
The report must be published
The report must include a review of compliance with sections 120A and 120B
The report must include a review of compliance with section 108 of the Deregulation Act 2: 2015
The report must include a review of compliance with section 21 of the Legislative and Regulatory Reform Act 2006

sections.source_quote: produce a general report on the carrying out of the Commissioner's functions annually

sections.heading: Publication by the Commissioner
sections.title: Publication requirements for Commissioner documents
sections.summary: The Commissioner must publish documents in a form and manner they consider appropriate when required by law. This section ensures that any documents the Commissioner is duty-bound to publish are made available in a manner that meets their standards.
sections.dtia_summary: This section impacts DTIAs by specifying how Commissioner publications must be handled, which affects transparency in data transfer processes.

requirements.text: The Commissioner must publish a document in such form and manner as they consider appropriate

sections.source_quote: A duty under this Act for the Commissioner to publish a document is a duty for the Commissioner to publish it, or to arrange for it to be published, in such form and manner as the Commissioner considers appropriate.

sections.heading: Notices from the Commissioner
sections.title: Methods for delivering notices to individuals and entities
sections.summary: This section specifies how notices authorized or required by the Data Protection Act 2018 may be delivered to individuals or entities. It outlines delivery methods including physical delivery to the individual's residence or business, delivery to a body corporate's principal office, and electronic delivery with consent. It also defines key terms such as 'principal office', 'proper officer', and 'registered company'.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines delivery methods for notices to individuals and entities, relevant for ensuring compliance with notice delivery requirements during data transfer impact assessments.

requirements.text: Notices may be delivered to an individual by delivering it to the individual
Notices may be delivered to an individual by sending it to the individual by post addressed to the individual at their usual or last-known place of residence or business
Notices may be delivered to an individual by leaving it for the individual at their place of residence or business
Notices may be delivered to a body corporate or unincorporate by sending it by post to the proper officer at its principal office
Notices may be delivered to a body corporate or unincorporate by addressing it to the proper officer and leaving it at that office
Notices may be delivered to a partnership in Scotland by sending it by post to the principal office of the partnership
Notices may be delivered to a partnership in Scotland by addressing it to that partnership and leaving it at that office
Notices may be delivered to a person by other means including electronic means with the person's consent

sections.source_quote: Notices from the Commissioner (1) This section applies in relation to a notice authorised or required by this Act to be given to a person by the Commissioner.

sections.heading: Information notices: restrictions
sections.title: Restrictions on information notices
sections.summary: This section outlines restrictions on information notices, including prohibitions on requiring information that would infringe parliamentary privileges, communications made by legal advisers, communications that could expose a person to criminal proceedings, and specific exclusions for certain types of information.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets restrictions on information notices, ensuring compliance with legal protections during data transfer impact assessments.

sections.heading: Information orders
sections.title: Information orders for non-compliance
sections.summary: This section allows courts to issue orders requiring individuals to provide information to the Commissioner if they fail to comply with an information notice. The orders specify the information required, form, timeframe, and place for providing information.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides mechanisms for enforcing compliance with information notices, critical for data transfer impact assessments.

requirements.text: Courts may require a person to provide information referred to in the information notice
Courts may require other information the Commissioner requires
Orders may specify the form of information to be provided
Orders must specify the time or period within which information must be provided
Orders may specify the place where information must be provided

sections.source_quote: Information orders for non-compliance (1) This section applies where a person has been given an information notice requiring the person to provide the Commissioner with information

sections.heading: Assessment notices: restrictions
sections.title: Restrictions on assessment notices
sections.summary: This section outlines restrictions on assessment notices, including prohibitions on requiring information that would infringe parliamentary privileges, disclosures of communications made by legal advisers, and specific exclusions for certain types of information.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets restrictions on assessment notices, ensuring compliance with legal protections during data transfer impact assessments.

requirements.text: Assessment notices do not require information that would infringe parliamentary privileges
Assessment notices do not have effect if compliance would disclose communications made by legal advisers
Assessment notices do not have effect if compliance would disclose communications that could expose a person to criminal proceedings

sections.source_quote: Assessment notices: restrictions (1) An assessment notice does not require a person to do something to the extent that requiring the person to do it would involve an infringement of the privileges of either House of Parliament.

sections.heading: Destroying or falsifying information and documents etc
sections.title: Offences related to destroying or falsifying information
sections.summary: This section defines offences for destroying, concealing, blocking, or falsifying information, documents, equipment, or materials when required by an information or assessment notice. It also provides a defence for destruction occurring without the notice.
sections.dtia_summary: Addresses destruction of information during data transfer impact assessments, critical for ensuring data integrity.

requirements.text: It is an offence to destroy or falsify information, documents, equipment, or materials with the intention of preventing the Commissioner from viewing them
It is a defence to prove destruction would have occurred without the notice

sections.source_quote: Destroying or falsifying information and documents etc (1) This section applies where a person has been given an information notice requiring the person to provide the Commissioner with information

sections.heading: Interview notices
sections.title: Interview notices: requirements for individuals
sections.summary: The Commissioner may issue written notices requiring individuals to attend and answer questions for investigations into suspected failures or offences by controllers or processors. Individuals covered include those directly involved in the controller/processor operations, their employees, or those managing the entity. The notice must specify the time of attendance, the nature of the suspected failure/offence, consequences of non-compliance, and rights under appeal procedures. Individuals cannot be required to attend before an appeal period ends, and may be exempt if an appeal is pending.
sections.dtia_summary: Interview notices require individuals to attend for investigations into suspected data protection failures; this section impacts DTIAs by defining who can be interviewed and when compliance is required, with specific time constraints and appeal protections.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may require an individual to attend at a specified place and answer questions relevant to an investigation
The notice must specify the time for attendance
The notice must indicate the nature of the suspected failure or offence
The notice must provide information about consequences of non-compliance
The notice must provide information about rights under sections 162 and 164
An individual cannot be required to attend before the end of the appeal period
If an appeal is pending, the individual need not attend pending appeal determination

sections.source_quote: 148A(2) For the purpose of investigating the suspected failure or offence, the Commissioner may, by written notice (an "interview notice"), require an individual within subsection (3) to— (a) attend at a place specified in the notice, and (b) answer questions with respect to any matter relevant to the investigation.

sections.heading: Interview notices: restrictions
sections.title: Interview notices: restrictions on questioning
sections.summary: Interview notices cannot require individuals to answer questions that would infringe parliamentary privileges, involve communications protected by legal advice, or expose individuals to criminal proceedings for offences under this Act. The notice may not be used for investigations of security bodies under the Freedom of Information Act 2000. Statements made in response may not be used in evidence against the individual unless inconsistent with the statement or evidence is adduced.
sections.dtia_summary: Interview notices are restricted from questioning individuals in certain contexts, such as legal advice, parliamentary privileges, or criminal proceedings, which impacts DTIAs by limiting the scope of investigations and potential evidence use.

requirements.text: Interview notices do not require individuals to answer questions that would infringe parliamentary privileges
Interview notices do not require individuals to answer questions about communications made with legal advisers
Interview notices do not require individuals to answer questions if doing so would expose them to criminal proceedings for offences under this Act
Interview notices may not be used for investigations of security bodies under the Freedom of Information Act 2000

sections.source_quote: 148B(1) An interview notice does not require an individual to answer questions to the extent that requiring the person to do so would involve an infringement of the privileges of either House of Parliament.

sections.heading: False statements made in response to interview notices
sections.title: False statements in interview notices
sections.summary: Making false statements in response to interview notices is an offence if the individual knows the statement is false or recklessly makes a false statement in a material respect. This section defines the prohibited conduct related to interview responses without specifying penalties or enforcement mechanisms.
sections.dtia_summary: False statements in interview responses are prohibited, which impacts DTIAs by defining when statements made during investigations could be considered unlawful.

requirements.text: It is an offence to make a statement which the individual knows to be false in a material respect
It is an offence to recklessly make a statement which is false in a material respect

sections.source_quote: 148C It is an offence for an individual, in response to an interview notice— (a) to make a statement which the individual knows to be false in a material respect, or (b) recklessly to make a statement which is false in a material respect.

sections.heading: Enforcement notices
sections.title: Enforcement notices: requirements for compliance
sections.summary: The Commissioner may issue enforcement notices requiring individuals to take steps or refrain from actions where a controller or processor has failed to comply with data protection principles, data subject rights, or obligations under GDPR or this Act. Enforcement notices must specify the failure, reasons for the notice, and may impose bans on data processing. The Commissioner may not require compliance before an appeal period ends, and urgent compliance is limited to 24 hours.
sections.dtia_summary: Enforcement notices require specific actions for data protection failures, impacting DTIAs by defining when and how data processing must be corrected or restricted across borders.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may issue enforcement notices requiring steps or refraining from actions
The enforcement notice must state what the person has failed or is failing to do
The enforcement notice must provide the Commissioner's reasons for the opinion
The Commissioner may impose a ban on all processing of personal data
The Commissioner may impose a ban on specific types of processing of personal data
The enforcement notice may specify time periods for compliance
The enforcement notice must not require compliance before the end of the appeal period
If an appeal is pending, the requirement need not be complied with pending appeal determination

sections.source_quote: 149(1) Where the Commissioner is satisfied that a person has failed, or is failing, as described in subsection (2), (3), (4) [F427, (5) or (5A)], the Commissioner may give the person a written notice (an "enforcement notice") which requires the person— (a) to take steps specified in the notice, or (b) to refrain from taking steps specified in the notice, or both (and see also sections 150 and 151).

sections.heading: Enforcement notices: supplementary
sections.title: Enforcement notices: supplementary requirements
sections.summary: Enforcement notices must state the specific failure and reasons for the notice. The Commissioner must consider if the failure has caused or likely to cause damage. The power to impose bans on data processing includes specifying data types, purposes, or timeframes. Compliance periods must not precede appeal deadlines, and urgent compliance is limited to 24 hours.
sections.dtia_summary: Enforcement notices have specific requirements for stating failures and reasons, which impact DTIAs by defining how data processing restrictions are applied in investigations.

requirements.text: The enforcement notice must state what the person has failed or is failing to do
The enforcement notice must provide the Commissioner's reasons for the opinion

The Commissioner may impose a ban on all processing of personal data
The Commissioner may impose a ban on specific types of processing of personal data

The enforcement notice must not require compliance before the end of the appeal period
If an appeal is pending, the requirement need not be complied with pending appeal determination

sections.source_quote: 150(1) An enforcement notice must— (a) state what the person has failed or is failing to do, and (b) give the Commissioner's reasons for reaching that opinion.

sections.heading: Enforcement notices: rectification and erasure of personal data etc
sections.title: Enforcement notices: rectification and erasure of personal data
sections.summary: Enforcement notices may require rectification or erasure of inaccurate personal data, including related data that appears to be based on inaccurate information. The Commissioner may require steps to ensure accuracy, secure data subject's view of inaccuracy, and supplement data with true facts. Notifications to third parties may be required if reasonably practicable.
sections.dtia_summary: Enforcement notices can mandate data rectification and erasure, impacting DTIAs by defining how inaccurate data must be corrected and notified to third parties.

requirements.text: The enforcement notice may require rectification or erasure of inaccurate personal data
The enforcement notice may require rectification or erasure of related data
The enforcement notice may require steps to ensure data accuracy
The enforcement notice may require securing the data subject's view of inaccuracy
The enforcement notice may require supplementing data with true facts
The Commissioner must consider the purpose for which data was obtained when determining steps
The Commissioner must consider the number of people to notify when determining if notification is reasonably practicable

sections.source_quote: 151(1) Subsections (2) and (3) apply where an enforcement notice is given in respect of a failure by a controller or processor— (a) to comply with a data protection principle relating to accuracy, or (b) to comply with a data subject's request to exercise rights under Article 16, 17 or 18 of the [F442UK GDPR] (right to rectification, erasure or restriction on processing) or section 46, 47 or 100 of this Act.

sections.heading: Enforcement notices: restrictions
sections.title: Restrictions on enforcement notices for data processing failures
sections.summary: The Commissioner cannot issue enforcement notices for data processing failures unless specific conditions are met. These include the notice being legally effective and a court granting leave for the notice. The court must be satisfied that the Commissioner suspects a substantial public importance failure and the controller or processor has been notified or the case is urgent. Enforcement notices do not require actions that would infringe parliamentary privileges. For joint controllers, the Commissioner may only issue notices if the controller is responsible for compliance with the relevant provision.
sections.dtia_summary: Restricts when enforcement notices can be issued for data processing failures, requiring court approval for substantial public importance cases and ensuring only responsible controllers are targeted. Relevant for assessing whether enforcement actions are legally permissible in cross-border data contexts.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may not give an enforcement notice in reliance on section 149(2) unless the notice has taken effect
A court must grant leave for the notice to be given only if the Commissioner suspects a failure of substantial public importance
A court must grant leave for the notice to be given only if the controller or processor has been notified or the case is urgent
Enforcement notices do not require actions that would infringe parliamentary privileges
For joint controllers, the Commissioner may only issue notices if the controller is responsible for compliance

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner may not give a controller or processor an enforcement notice in reliance on section 149(2) with respect to the processing of personal data for the special purposes unless— (a) has taken effect, and (b) a court has granted leave for the notice to be given.

sections.heading: Enforcement notices: cancellation and variation
sections.title: Cancellation and variation of enforcement notices
sections.summary: The Commissioner can cancel or vary enforcement notices by written notice to the affected party. The affected party may apply in writing for cancellation or variation. Applications can only be made after the appeal period ends and if changes in circumstances mean the notice provisions no longer need compliance to remedy the identified failure.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for modification or termination of enforcement notices based on changed circumstances, relevant for assessing when data transfer restrictions can be adjusted without legal challenges.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may cancel or vary an enforcement notice by written notice to the person
A person may apply in writing to the Commissioner for cancellation or variation of the notice

An application may only be made if changes in circumstances mean the notice provisions no longer need compliance to remedy the failure

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner may cancel or vary an enforcement notice by giving written notice to the person to whom it was given.

sections.heading: Penalty notices
sections.title: Penalty notices for data processing failures
sections.summary: The Commissioner may issue penalty notices requiring payment for failures to comply with data protection obligations. The penalty amount must consider the nature of the failure, intent, mitigation efforts, technical measures, previous failures, cooperation with the Commissioner, data categories affected, and whether the penalty is effective and proportionate. Penalties apply to failures described in sections 149(2), (3), (4), (5), or (5A), and non-compliance with notices. The Secretary of State may regulate penalties for other failures.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines penalty structures for data processing failures, including factors to consider for proportionality and effectiveness, critical for assessing financial impacts of non-compliance in cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may require payment of a penalty for failures to comply with data protection obligations
The penalty amount must consider the nature, gravity, and duration of the failure
The penalty amount must consider the intentional or negligent character of the failure
The penalty amount must consider actions taken to mitigate damage or distress
The penalty amount must consider technical and organizational measures implemented
The penalty amount must consider previous failures by the controller or processor
The penalty amount must consider the degree of cooperation with the Commissioner
The penalty amount must consider the categories of personal data affected
The penalty amount must consider how the infringement became known to the Commissioner
The penalty amount must consider compliance with previous enforcement notices
The penalty amount must consider adherence to approved codes of conduct
The penalty amount must be effective, proportionate, and dissuasive

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner may, by written notice (a "penalty notice"), require the person to pay to the Commissioner an amount in sterling specified in the notice.

sections.heading: Penalty notices: restrictions
sections.title: Restrictions on penalty notices for data processing failures
sections.summary: The Commissioner cannot issue penalty notices for data processing failures unless specific conditions are met. These include the notice being legally effective and court approval for substantial public importance failures. Penalties do not apply to processing where purposes are determined by Parliament. The Commissioner cannot issue notices to the Crown Estate Commissioners or persons acting as controllers for the Royal Household. For joint controllers, the Commissioner may only issue notices if the controller is responsible for compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets conditions for penalty notices, including court approval for substantial public importance cases and exclusions for specific entities, relevant for assessing when penalties apply in cross-border data contexts.

requirements.text: The Commissioner may not issue penalty notices unless the notice has taken effect
