The Commissioner may not issue penalty notices to persons acting as controllers for the Royal Household
For joint controllers, the Commissioner may only issue notices if the controller is responsible for compliance

sections.source_quote: The Commissioner may not give a controller or processor a penalty notice in reliance on section 149(2) with respect to the processing of personal data for the special purposes unless— (a) has taken effect, and (b) a court has granted leave for the notice to be given.

sections.heading: Maximum amount of penalty
sections.title: Maximum penalty amounts for data processing failures
sections.summary: Penalties for data processing failures are capped at specific amounts based on the type of failure. For GDPR violations, penalties are set by Article 83 of the GDPR. For other failures, penalties are either the 'higher maximum amount' (up to 4% of global turnover) or 'standard maximum amount' (up to 2% of global turnover). The 'higher maximum amount' applies to certain serious failures, while the 'standard maximum amount' applies to others.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies maximum penalty amounts for data processing failures, critical for assessing financial risks and compliance costs in cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Penalties for GDPR violations are capped at the amount specified in Article 83 of the GDPR

sections.source_quote: In relation to an infringement of a provision of the [F449UK GDPR], the maximum amount of the penalty that may be imposed by a penalty notice is— (a) the amount specified in Article 83 of the [F449UK GDPR], or (b) if an amount is not specified there, the standard maximum amount.

sections.heading: Fixed penalties for non-compliance with charges regulations
sections.title: Fixed penalties for non-compliance with charges regulations
sections.summary: The Commissioner must publish fixed penalty amounts for failures to comply with charges regulations. Different penalties can be specified for different types of failures. The maximum penalty is 150% of the highest charge payable by a controller in a financial year. The Commissioner must consult the Secretary of State and lay the document before Parliament before publishing.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets fixed penalty structures for regulatory compliance failures, relevant for assessing financial penalties in cross-border data transfer contexts.

sections.heading: Amount of penalties: supplementary
sections.title: Regulatory definitions for penalty calculations
sections.summary: This section allows the Secretary of State to create regulations defining specific types of undertakings and methods for determining an undertaking's turnover for penalty calculations under GDPR. It also permits regulations specifying financial year periods for penalty assessments. The regulations must align with GDPR Article 83 and section 157.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines how penalties are calculated for cross-border data transfers by specifying undertaking types and turnover determination methods, relevant for DTIA when assessing penalty impacts.

sections.heading: Guidance about regulatory action

sections.summary: This section mandates that the Commissioner publish guidance on how they propose to exercise their functions regarding information notices, assessment notices, interview notices, and penalty notices. The guidance must specify factors for determining notice timing, circumstances for urgent cases, and procedures for non-compliance. Additional guidance may be produced for other functions under this Part.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires the Commissioner to publish detailed guidance on regulatory actions, including notice types and procedures, which impacts DTIA by defining how regulatory notices are issued and enforced across borders.

requirements.text: Commissioner must produce and publish guidance about how the Commissioner proposes to exercise the Commissioner's functions in connection with information notices
Commissioner must produce and publish guidance about how the Commissioner proposes to exercise the Commissioner's functions in connection with assessment notices
Commissioner must produce and publish guidance about how the Commissioner proposes to exercise the Commissioner's functions in connection with interview notices
Commissioner must produce and publish guidance about how the Commissioner proposes to exercise the Commissioner's functions in connection with enforcement notices
Commissioner must produce and publish guidance about how the Commissioner proposes to exercise the Commissioner's other functions under this Part
Guidance must include provision specifying factors to be considered in determining the time at which, or the period within which, information is to be required to be provided
Guidance must include provision about the circumstances in which the Commissioner would consider it appropriate to give an information notice to a person in reliance on section 142(7) (urgent cases)
Guidance must include provision about how the Commissioner will determine how to proceed if a person does not comply with an information notice
Guidance must include provision specifying factors to be considered in determining whether to give an assessment notice to a person
Guidance must include provision specifying factors to be considered in determining whether to give an assessment notice to a person that imposes a requirement of a sort mentioned in section 146(2)(j)
Guidance must include provision about the factors the Commissioner may take into account when determining the suitability of a person to prepare a report of a sort mentioned in section 146(2)(j)
Guidance must include provision about the circumstances in which the Commissioner would consider it appropriate to give an assessment notice in reliance on section 146(8) or (9) (urgent cases)
Guidance must include provision specifying descriptions of documents or information that are not to be examined or inspected in accordance with an assessment notice
Guidance must include provision specifying descriptions of documents or information that are to be examined or inspected only by a person of a description specified in the guidance
Guidance must include provision about the nature of inspections and examinations carried out in accordance with an assessment notice
Guidance must include provision about the nature of interviews carried out in accordance with an assessment notice

Guidance must include provision about the preparation, issuing and publication by the Commissioner of assessment reports in respect of controllers and processors that have been given assessment notices
Guidance must include provision about how the Commissioner will determine how to proceed if a person does not comply with an assessment notice
Guidance must include provisions that relate to documents and information concerning an individual's physical or mental health
Guidance must include provisions that relate to documents and information concerning the provision of social care for an individual

sections.source_quote: 160 (1) The Commissioner must produce and publish guidance about how the Commissioner proposes to exercise the Commissioner's functions in connection with— (a) information notices, (b) assessment notices, (c) interview notices, (d) enforcement notices, and (e) penalty notices.

sections.heading: Approval of first guidance about regulatory action
sections.title: Approval of first regulatory guidance
sections.summary: This section requires the Commissioner to submit the first guidance produced under section 160 to the Secretary of State, who must lay it before Parliament. If Parliament within 40 days rejects the guidance, the Commissioner must produce a revised version. If approved, the guidance takes effect after 21 days from issuance.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires parliamentary approval for regulatory guidance before implementation, which impacts DTIA by ensuring regulatory actions are legally validated before affecting data transfers.

requirements.text: Commissioner must submit the final version of guidance to the Secretary of State
Secretary of State must lay the guidance before Parliament
If Parliament resolves not to approve the guidance within 40 days, Commissioner must not issue the guidance
If Parliament resolves not to approve the guidance within 40 days, Commissioner must produce another version of the guidance
If no resolution is made within 40 days, Commissioner must issue the guidance
If no resolution is made within 40 days, guidance comes into force 21 days after issuance

sections.source_quote: 161 (1) When the first guidance is produced under section 160(1)— (a) the Commissioner must submit the final version to the Secretary of State, and (b) the Secretary of State must lay the guidance before Parliament.

sections.heading: Rights of appeal
sections.title: Appeal rights for regulatory notices
sections.summary: This section grants appeal rights to individuals who receive information notices, assessment notices, interview notices, enforcement notices, or penalty notices. Appeals can be made against the notice itself or specific aspects like penalty amounts, with the Tribunal having authority to review determinations of fact or exercise discretion.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a mechanism for challenging regulatory notices, which impacts DTIA by allowing data subjects to contest notices affecting data transfers.

requirements.text: A person given an information notice may appeal to the Tribunal
A person given an assessment notice may appeal to the Tribunal
A person given an interview notice may appeal to the Tribunal
A person given an enforcement notice may appeal to the Tribunal
A person given a penalty notice may appeal to the Tribunal
A person given a penalty variation notice may appeal to the Tribunal
A person given an enforcement notice may appeal to the Tribunal against the refusal of an application under section 153 for the cancellation or variation of the notice
A person given a penalty notice or penalty variation notice may appeal to the Tribunal against the amount of the penalty specified in the notice

sections.source_quote: 162 (1) A person who is given any of the following notices may appeal to the Tribunal— (a) an information notice; (b) an assessment notice; (c) an interview notice; (d) an enforcement notice; (e) a penalty notice; (f) a penalty variation notice.

sections.heading: Determination of appeals
sections.title: Tribunal determination of appeals
sections.summary: This section outlines the Tribunal's authority to review determinations of fact, allow appeals if notices are unlawful or if discretion was exercised incorrectly, and substitute notices or decisions. The Tribunal may also cancel enforcement notices if circumstances change.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines how appeals are handled by the Tribunal, which impacts DTIA by providing a mechanism for correcting regulatory notices affecting data transfers.

requirements.text: Tribunal may review any determination of fact on which the notice or decision against which the appeal is brought was based
If Tribunal considers the notice or decision is not in accordance with the law, Tribunal must allow the appeal or substitute another notice or decision
Otherwise, Tribunal must dismiss the appeal
On an appeal under section 162(2), if Tribunal considers the enforcement notice ought to be cancelled or varied by reason of a change in circumstances, Tribunal must cancel or vary the notice
On an appeal under section 162(4), Tribunal may cancel the Commissioner's determination

sections.source_quote: 163 (1) Subsections (2) to (4) apply where a person appeals to the Tribunal under section 162(1) or (3). (2) The Tribunal may review any determination of fact on which the notice or decision against which the appeal is brought was based. (3) If the Tribunal considers— (a) that the notice or decision against which the appeal is brought is not in accordance with the law, or (b) to the extent that the notice or decision involved an exercise of discretion by the Commissioner, that the Commissioner ought to have exercised the discretion differently, the Tribunal must allow the appeal or substitute another notice or decision which the Commissioner could have given or made.

sections.heading: Applications in respect of urgent notices
sections.title: Urgent notices applications
sections.summary: This section allows individuals to apply to the court to disapply urgency statements in notices or vary compliance timelines. The court may direct that the notice has no effect with the urgency statement, vary the time period for compliance, or make other changes to satisfy the application.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides a process for challenging the urgency of regulatory notices, which impacts DTIA by allowing adjustments to time-sensitive data transfer requirements.

requirements.text: A person may apply to the court for disapplication of the urgency statement in relation to some or all of the requirements of the notice
A person may apply to the court for a change to the time at which, or the period within which, a requirement of the notice must be complied with
On an application, the court may direct that the notice is to have effect as if it did not contain the urgency statement
On an application, the court may direct that the inclusion of the urgency statement is not to have effect in relation to a requirement of the notice
On an application, the court may vary the notice by changing the time at which, or the period within which, a requirement of the notice must be complied with

sections.source_quote: 164 (1) This section applies where an information notice, an assessment notice or an enforcement notice given to a person contains an urgency statement. (2) The person may apply to the court for either or both of the following— (a) the disapplication of the urgency statement in relation to some or all of the requirements of the notice; (b) a change to the time at which, or the period within which, a requirement of the notice must be complied with.

sections.heading: Controllers to notify the Commissioner of the number of complaints
sections.title: Controllers must report complaint volumes to Commissioner
sections.summary: Regulations may require controllers to notify the Commissioner of complaint volumes made under section 164A, specifying periods and calculation methods. Controllers must comply with notification requirements set by regulations, including form, timing, and complaint counting procedures. The Commissioner has authority to determine these requirements through regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: Controllers must report complaint volumes to the Commissioner, which affects data transfer impact assessments by requiring transparency in complaint handling processes across borders.

requirements.text: Controllers must notify the Commissioner of complaint volumes in periods specified by regulations
Regulations may specify when notifications must be made
Regulations may define how complaint volumes are calculated

sections.source_quote: Controllers to notify the Commissioner of the number of complaints made to the controller under section 164A in periods specified or described in the regulations.

sections.heading: Complaints by data subjects [F470to the Commissioner]
sections.title: Data subjects can complain to Commissioner about data protection breaches
sections.summary: Data subjects may complain to the Commissioner if they believe their personal data processing violates UK GDPR or Part 3/4 of the Act. The Commissioner must facilitate complaints through electronic forms and other means. The Commissioner must respond to complaints, inform complainants of outcomes, and provide information about further rights within 3 months of receiving a complaint.
sections.dtia_summary: Data subjects can complain to the Commissioner about data processing breaches, which impacts DTIAs by requiring organizations to have mechanisms for handling cross-border complaints and ensuring timely responses.

requirements.text: Data subjects may make complaints to the Commissioner if they believe personal data processing violates UK GDPR or Part 3/4 of the Act
Commissioner must provide electronic complaint forms and other means to facilitate complaints
Commissioner must respond to complaints within 3 months of receiving them
Commissioner must inform complainants of complaint outcomes within 3 months
Commissioner must inform complainants of their rights under section 166
Commissioner must provide further information about complaint progression if requested

sections.source_quote: A data subject may make a complaint to the Commissioner if the data subject considers that, in connection with personal data relating to him or her, there is an infringement of the UK GDPR or Part 3 or 4 of this Act.

sections.heading: Orders to progress complaints [F476to the Commissioner]
sections.title: Tribunal can order Commissioner to respond to complaints
sections.summary: If the Commissioner fails to respond to complaints within 3 months, a tribunal may order the Commissioner to take appropriate steps or inform complainants of progress within a specified period. The order may require the Commissioner to conclude investigations or take specific actions within the timeframe set by the tribunal. This section applies to complaints made under section 165.
sections.dtia_summary: Tribunals can order the Commissioner to respond to complaints, which affects DTIAs by requiring organizations to ensure timely complaint resolution and transparency in cross-border data processing.

requirements.text: Tribunal may order Commissioner to take appropriate steps to respond to complaints
Tribunal may order Commissioner to inform complainants of progress within a specified period
Commissioner must conclude investigations within the timeframe specified in the order

sections.source_quote: The Tribunal may, on an application by the data subject, make an order requiring the Commissioner to take appropriate steps to respond to the complaint.

sections.heading: Compliance orders
sections.title: Courts can issue compliance orders for data protection breaches
sections.summary: Courts may issue compliance orders requiring controllers or processors to take specific steps or refrain from actions to comply with data protection legislation. The orders specify timeframes for actions and apply to breaches of data subject rights. Joint controllers must be responsible for compliance with the specific provision that was contravened.
sections.dtia_summary: Courts can issue compliance orders for data protection breaches, which impacts DTIAs by requiring organizations to ensure compliance with cross-border data processing obligations.

requirements.text: Courts may order controllers or processors to take specific steps to comply with data protection legislation
Courts may order controllers or processors to refrain from taking specific steps
Orders may specify timeframes for taking steps
Joint controllers must be responsible for compliance with the specific provision that was contravened

sections.source_quote: A court may make an order for the purposes of securing compliance with the data protection legislation which requires the controller in respect of the processing, or a processor acting on behalf of that controller—

sections.heading: Compensation for contravention of the [F479UK GDPR]
sections.title: Compensation for distress caused by data breaches
sections.summary: The UK GDPR includes 'distress' as non-material damage for which compensation may be awarded. Courts may order compensation to be paid to representative bodies or individuals affected by data breaches, provided the breach was caused by contravention of data protection legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: Compensation for distress caused by data breaches is relevant to DTIAs as it affects the financial impact of data processing incidents across borders.

requirements.text: Distress is included as non-material damage for compensation under the UK GDPR

sections.source_quote: In Article 82 of the UK GDPR (right to compensation for material or non-material damage), 'non-material damage' includes distress.

sections.heading: Compensation for contravention of other data protection legislation
sections.title: Compensation for data protection breaches
sections.summary: Individuals who suffer damage due to breaches of data protection legislation (other than UK GDPR) are entitled to compensation from controllers or processors. Controllers are liable for all damage, while processors are liable only if they failed to comply with specific obligations or acted contrary to the controller's instructions. Joint controllers share liability based on their specific responsibilities.
sections.dtia_summary: Compensation for data protection breaches impacts DTIAs by requiring organizations to assess financial and non-financial risks associated with cross-border data processing incidents.

requirements.text: Individuals suffering damage due to data protection breaches are entitled to compensation from controllers or processors
Controllers are liable for all damage caused by processing
Processors are liable only if they failed to comply with specific obligations or acted contrary to the controller's instructions
Joint controllers share liability based on their specific responsibilities

sections.source_quote: A person who suffers damage by reason of a contravention of a requirement of the data protection legislation, other than the UK GDPR, is entitled to compensation for that damage from the controller or the processor, subject to subsections (2) and (3).

sections.heading: Unlawful obtaining etc of personal data
sections.title: Unlawful transfer of personal data without consent
sections.summary: This section prohibits knowingly or recklessly obtaining, disclosing, or retaining personal data without the controller's consent. It also prohibits selling personal data obtained unlawfully. The offence includes specific defences related to crime prevention, public interest, and reasonable belief in lawful actions. The section clarifies that consent references exclude certain processors and multi-controller scenarios.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes restrictions on cross-border data transfers by prohibiting unlawful data transfers without consent, requiring consent for data transfers, and defining defences for lawful processing in specific contexts.

requirements.text: Must not obtain personal data without controller's consent
Must not disclose personal data to another person without controller's consent
Must not retain personal data without the person who was the controller's consent
Must not sell personal data obtained in circumstances where an offence under subsection (1) was committed
Must not offer to sell personal data obtained in circumstances where an offence under subsection (1) was committed

sections.source_quote: It is an offence for a person knowingly or recklessly—(a) to obtain or disclose personal data without the consent of the controller, (b) to procure the disclosure of personal data to another person without the consent of the controller, or (c) after obtaining personal data, to retain it without the consent of the person who was the controller in relation to the personal data when it was obtained.

sections.heading: Re-identification of de-identified personal data
sections.title: Re-identification of de-identified personal data
sections.summary: This section prohibits re-identifying de-identified personal data without the controller's consent. It defines de-identified data as data that cannot be attributed to a specific data subject. The offence includes defences related to crime prevention, public interest, and reasonable belief in lawful actions. It also prohibits processing re-identified data without consent or in circumstances where re-identification was unlawful.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses re-identification risks in cross-border data transfers by requiring consent for re-identification, defining de-identified data, and imposing conditions for lawful re-identification without consent.

requirements.text: Must not re-identify de-identified personal data without controller's consent
De-identified personal data is data that can no longer be attributed to a specific data subject
Re-identification occurs when steps result in information no longer being de-identified
Must not process re-identified data without controller's consent
Must not process re-identified data in circumstances where re-identification was an offence

sections.source_quote: It is an offence for a person knowingly or recklessly to re-identify information that is de-identified personal data without the consent of the controller responsible for de-identifying the personal data.

sections.heading: Re-identification: effectiveness testing conditions
sections.title: Effectiveness testing conditions for re-identification
sections.summary: This section defines the effectiveness testing conditions required for re-identification of de-identified personal data. It requires that re-identification must be done with a view to testing de-identification effectiveness, without intending harm, and in the public interest. The re-identifier must also notify the Commissioner or controller within 72 hours of becoming aware of the re-identification.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies conditions for lawful re-identification of de-identified data, including notification requirements and public interest justification, which are critical for DTIA assessments involving data re-identification.

requirements.text: Must act with a view to testing de-identification effectiveness
Must not intend to cause or threaten damage or distress
Must act in reasonable belief that re-identification is justified in public interest
Must notify Commissioner or controller within 72 hours of re-identification

sections.source_quote: The first condition is that the person acted—(a) with a view to testing the effectiveness of the de-identification of personal data, (b) without intending to cause, or threaten to cause, damage or distress to a person, and (c) in the reasonable belief that, in the particular circumstances, re-identifying the information was justified as being in the public interest.

sections.heading: Alteration etc of personal data to prevent disclosure to data subject
sections.title: Altering personal data to prevent disclosure to data subject
sections.summary: This section prohibits altering, defacing, blocking, erasing, destroying, or concealing information to prevent disclosure to a data subject when the data subject has exercised a right to access their information. It applies to controllers and their employees who act under the controller's direction. The offence includes defences related to the absence of a data subject access request or reasonable belief that the data subject is not entitled to the information.
sections.dtia_summary: This section addresses data sharing restrictions by prohibiting alterations to prevent data disclosure to data subjects, which is relevant for DTIA assessments involving data sharing practices.

requirements.text: Must not alter, deface, block, erase, destroy, or conceal information to prevent disclosure to data subject
Data subject access right includes rights under GDPR Articles 15 and 20

sections.source_quote: It is an offence for a person listed in subsection (4) to alter, deface, block, erase, destroy or conceal information with the intention of preventing disclosure of all or part of the information that the person making the request would have been entitled to receive.

sections.heading: The special purposes
sections.title: Special purposes for data processing
sections.summary: This section defines special purposes such as journalism, academic, artistic, and literary purposes. It allows the Commissioner to determine that personal data is not processed only for special purposes or not intended for publication. The determination requires written notice to the controller and processor and takes effect after an appeal period ends.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides a framework for data processing in special purposes, which can be relevant for DTIA assessments involving cross-border data transfers for journalistic or academic activities.

requirements.text: Special purposes include journalism, academic, artistic, and literary purposes
Special purposes proceedings relate to legal actions involving personal data processed for special purposes
Commissioner may determine personal data is not processed only for special purposes
Commissioner may determine personal data is not processed for publication of unpublished material

sections.source_quote: In this Part, 'the special purposes' means one or more of the following—(a) the purposes of journalism; (b) academic purposes; (c) artistic purposes; (d) literary purposes.

sections.heading: Provision of assistance in special purposes proceedings
sections.title: Assistance in special purposes proceedings
sections.summary: This section allows applicants in special purposes proceedings to request assistance from the Commissioner. The Commissioner must decide whether to grant assistance, which may include paying costs and indemnifying against liability. The assistance is subject to the case involving substantial public importance and requires notification to the applicant and the opposing party.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides mechanisms for assistance in special purposes proceedings, which can impact DTIA assessments by enabling legal proceedings involving cross-border data transfers for journalistic or academic purposes.

requirements.text: Commissioner must decide whether to grant assistance in special purposes proceedings
Assistance may include paying costs and indemnifying against liability
Case must involve substantial public importance for assistance to be granted

sections.source_quote: An individual who is a party, or prospective party, to special purposes proceedings may apply to the Commissioner for assistance in those proceedings.

sections.heading: Staying special purposes proceedings
sections.title: Staying special purposes proceedings in data protection cases
sections.summary: Courts must stay special purposes proceedings if the controller or processor claims personal data is processed only for special purposes, is being processed with a view to journalistic publication, and has not previously been published. Publication within the last 24 hours is disregarded. Proceedings remain stayed until either a Commissioner determination under section 174 takes effect or the claim is withdrawn.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires courts to stay proceedings involving personal data for journalistic purposes until compliance with data protection standards is confirmed, impacting cross-border data transfers where journalistic activities are involved.

requirements.text: Courts must stay special purposes proceedings if controller or processor claims personal data is processed only for special purposes
Courts must stay special purposes proceedings if personal data is processed with a view to journalistic publication
Courts must stay special purposes proceedings if personal data has not previously been published
Publication within the last 24 hours is disregarded when determining if data has been published
Proceedings must stay until either a Commissioner determination under section 174 takes effect or the claim is withdrawn

sections.source_quote: 176 Staying special purposes proceedings (1) In any special purposes proceedings before a court, if the controller or processor claims, or it appears to the court, that any personal data to which the proceedings relate—(a) is being processed only for the special purposes, (b) is being processed with a view to the publication by any person of journalistic, (c) has not previously been published by the controller, the court must stay or, in Scotland, sist the proceedings.

sections.heading: Guidance about how to seek redress against media organisations
sections.title: Guidance for media organizations data compliance complaints
sections.summary: The Commissioner must publish guidance on steps individuals can take to seek redress against media organizations failing data protection laws. The guidance must cover complaint procedures, Commissioner powers, court claims, alternative dispute resolution, and rights of organizations to act on behalf of data subjects. The first guidance must be published within one year of the Act's passage.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates the Commissioner to provide clear complaint procedures for media organizations, ensuring data subjects can effectively challenge breaches without cross-border data transfer complications.

requirements.text: Commissioner must produce and publish guidance on steps for individuals to seek redress against media organizations failing data protection laws
Guidance must include who runs complaint procedures
Guidance must specify what can be complained about
Guidance must detail how to make a complaint
Relevant complaints procedures include those for the Commissioner, Office of Communications, BBC, and others enforcing media codes
Guidance must cover Commissioner's powers in cases of data protection law violations
Guidance must explain when and how to make court claims for data protection violations
Guidance must include alternative dispute resolution procedures
Guidance must address rights of bodies to make complaints and claims on behalf of data subjects
Guidance must cover Commissioner's power to assist in special purpose proceedings
First guidance must be published within one year of the Act's passage

sections.source_quote: 177 Guidance about how to seek redress against media organisations (1) The Commissioner must produce and publish guidance about the steps that may be taken where an individual considers that a media organisation is failing or has failed to comply with the data protection legislation.

sections.heading: Review of processing of personal data for the purposes of journalism
sections.title: Review of journalism data processing compliance
sections.summary: The Commissioner must review personal data processing for journalistic purposes every four years, with subsequent five-year reviews. The review assesses compliance with good practice, prepares a report covering UK-wide compliance, and submits it to the Secretary of State within specific timeframes. The report must be laid before Parliament and sent to Scottish, Welsh, and Northern Irish authorities.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires regular reviews of journalism data processing to ensure compliance with data protection standards, affecting how media organizations handle personal data across borders.

requirements.text: Commissioner must review personal data processing for journalistic purposes during each review period
Commissioner must prepare a report of the review
Commissioner must submit the report to the Secretary of State within 18 months for the first review
Commissioner must submit the report within 12 months for subsequent reviews
Report must include consideration of compliance across all parts of the UK
Secretary of State must lay the report before Parliament
Secretary of State must send a copy of the report to Scottish Ministers, Welsh Ministers, and Northern Ireland Executive Office

sections.source_quote: 178 Review of processing of personal data for the purposes of journalism (a) review the extent to which, during each review period, the processing of personal data for the purposes of journalism complied with—(ii) good practice in the processing of personal data for the purposes of journalism, (b) prepare a report of the review, and (c) submit the report to the Secretary of State.

sections.heading: Effectiveness of the media's dispute resolution procedures
sections.title: Effectiveness of media dispute resolution procedures
sections.summary: The Secretary of State must report to Parliament on the use and effectiveness of alternative dispute resolution procedures for media organizations failing data protection laws. The report must cover cases involving relevant media organizations, with the Secretary of State sending copies to Scottish, Welsh, and Northern Irish authorities. The review period is three years, with subsequent three-year reviews.
sections.dtia_summary: This section requires reporting on dispute resolution effectiveness for media organizations, ensuring data protection compliance without cross-border data transfer issues.

requirements.text: Secretary of State must report to Parliament on use of alternative dispute resolution procedures during each review period
Secretary of State must report on effectiveness of alternative dispute resolution procedures
Secretary of State must send copies of reports to Scottish Ministers, Welsh Ministers, and Northern Ireland Executive Office

sections.source_quote: 179 Effectiveness of the media's dispute resolution procedures (1) The Secretary of State must, before the end of each review period, lay before Parliament a report produced by the Secretary of State or an appropriate person on—(a) the use of relevant alternative dispute resolution procedures, during that period, in cases involving a failure, or alleged failure, by a relevant media organisation to comply with the data protection legislation, and (b) the effectiveness of those procedures in such cases.

sections.heading: Jurisdiction
sections.title: Jurisdiction for data protection court proceedings
sections.summary: Courts have jurisdiction over data protection proceedings in England and Wales, Northern Ireland, and Scotland. Specific courts handle different types of data protection notices, with the High Court or Court of Session exercising jurisdiction for most cases. Jurisdiction for information notices with statements under section 142(7) is limited to the High Court or Court of Session.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines court jurisdiction for data protection cases, ensuring consistent handling of cross-border data transfers without conflicting legal authority.

requirements.text: Courts may exercise jurisdiction in England and Wales via High Court or county court
Courts may exercise jurisdiction in Northern Ireland via High Court or county court
Courts may exercise jurisdiction in Scotland via Court of Session or sheriff
Jurisdiction for information orders is limited to High Court or Court of Session
Jurisdiction for enforcement notices and special purposes processing is limited to High Court or Court of Session
Jurisdiction for penalty notices and special purposes processing is limited to High Court or Court of Session
Jurisdiction for compliance orders is limited to High Court or Court of Session
Jurisdiction for compensation claims is limited to High Court or Court of Session

sections.source_quote: 180 Jurisdiction (1) The jurisdiction conferred on a court by the provisions listed in subsection (2) is exercisable—(a) in England and Wales, by the High Court or the county court, (b) in Northern Ireland, by the High Court or a county court, and (c) in Scotland, by the Court of Session or the sheriff, subject to subsections (3) and (4).

sections.heading: Interpretation of Part 6
sections.title: Definitions for Part 6 of Data Protection Act
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in Part 6 of the Data Protection Act, including 'assessment notice', 'certification provider', 'enforcement notice', 'information notice', 'interview notice', 'penalty notice', and 'penalty variation notice'. These definitions support the implementation of data protection procedures and notices.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides definitions for data protection notices, ensuring clarity in how courts and authorities handle data transfers and processing.

requirements.text: Assessment notice has the meaning given in section 146
Certification provider has the meaning given in section 17
Enforcement notice has the meaning given in section 149
Information notice has the meaning given in section 142
Interview notice has the meaning given in section 148A
Penalty notice has the meaning given in section 155
Penalty variation notice has the meaning given in Schedule 16

sections.source_quote: 181 Interpretation of Part 6 In this Part—"assessment notice" has the meaning given in section 146;"certification provider" has the meaning given in section 17;"enforcement notice" has the meaning given in section 149;"information notice" has the meaning given in section 142;

sections.heading: Regulations and consultation
sections.title: Regulations must be made by statutory instrument with pre-consultation
sections.summary: Regulations under this Act must be created via statutory instrument. Before finalizing, the Secretary of State must consult the Commissioner and other appropriate parties. This provision does not apply to regulations made under specific sections (30, 211-213, and Schedule 2 paragraph 15). Regulations may include different provisions for different purposes and consequential amendments. The statutory instrument may be subject to negative or affirmative resolution procedures, affecting parliamentary approval requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulations for data protection must follow specific parliamentary approval procedures and consultation requirements, which impact how data transfer rules are implemented across borders.

requirements.text: Regulations under this Act are to be made by statutory instrument
Secretary of State must consult Commissioner before making regulations
Subsection (2) does not apply to regulations made under section 30
Subsection (2) does not apply to regulations made under section 211
Subsection (2) does not apply to regulations made under section 212
Subsection (2) does not apply to regulations made under section 213
Subsection (2) does not apply to regulations made under Schedule 2 paragraph 15
Regulations may make different provision for different purposes
Regulations may include consequential, supplementary, incidental, transitional, transitory or saving provision
Statutory instrument subject to negative resolution procedure may be annulled by parliamentary resolution
Statutory instrument subject to affirmative resolution procedure requires parliamentary approval

sections.heading: Power to reflect changes to the Data Protection Convention
sections.title: Prohibition on requiring access to relevant records for employment
sections.summary: It is illegal to require another person to provide or give access to relevant records in connection with employee recruitment, continued employment, or service contracts. The requirement must not be justified as being in the public interest for crime prevention. The term 'relevant record' includes parts of records and copies of records.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibits requiring access to personal records for employment purposes, protecting data subjects from unnecessary record sharing.

requirements.text: It is an offence to require another person to provide relevant records for employee recruitment
It is an offence to require another person to provide relevant records for continued employment
It is an offence to require another person to provide relevant records for service contracts
The requirement is not justified as being in the public interest for crime prevention
Relevant record includes parts of records and copies of records

sections.heading: Protection of prohibitions and restrictions etc on processing: relevant
Prohibition of requirement to produce relevant records
sections.title: Avoidance of contractual terms requiring health records
sections.summary: Contract terms requiring individuals to supply health records obtained through data subject access rights are void. This includes both supplying records and producing records. The term 'data subject access right' refers to rights under the UK GDPR for access and data portability.
sections.dtia_summary: Prevents contractual terms that require individuals to share health records obtained through data subject access rights, protecting health information.

requirements.text: Contract terms requiring supply of health records obtained through data subject access rights are void
Contract terms requiring production of health records obtained through data subject access rights are void
Relevant record includes parts of records and copies of records

sections.heading: Avoidance of certain contractual terms relating to health records
sections.title: Protection of data subject's rights from disclosure restrictions
sections.summary: Enactments or rules prohibiting disclosure of information or authorizing withholding of information do not remove or restrict data subject obligations and rights. The data subject rights are defined by the UK GDPR and specific provisions of this Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures data subject rights remain protected even when there are restrictions on information disclosure.

requirements.text: Enactments or rules prohibiting disclosure of information do not remove data subject obligations
Enactments or rules authorizing withholding of information do not restrict data subject rights
Data subject access rights include rights under UK GDPR for access and data portability

sections.heading: [F519Protection of data subject's rights]
Representation of data subjects with their authority
sections.title: Representation of data subjects with authority to exercise rights
sections.summary: This section allows authorized representative bodies to exercise data subjects' rights under the UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018. These bodies must meet specific conditions including charitable status, public interest objectives, and active data protection work. They can act on behalf of data subjects for complaints, judicial remedies, and compensation claims under the GDPR.
sections.dtia_summary: Represents a mechanism for data subjects to delegate rights to third parties, relevant for DTIAs involving data transfer where representatives act on behalf of individuals across borders.

requirements.text: A body or organisation must meet conditions in subsections (3) and (4) to exercise data subject rights

The body must prohibit distributing assets among members except for charitable/public purposes
The body must have public interest objectives
The body must be active in protecting data subjects' rights and freedoms
Representative bodies may exercise data subject rights under Article 80(1) of UK GDPR
Representative bodies may exercise data subject rights under Article 82 of UK GDPR

sections.heading: Representation of data subjects with their authority: collective proceedings
sections.title: Representative bodies can bring collective data subject claims
sections.summary: This section enables representative bodies to file collective legal proceedings in courts or tribunals for data subjects. It specifies that relevant claims include rights under Article 80(1) of the UK GDPR or section 187. The Secretary of State may regulate these proceedings, including settlement agreements, compensation assessments, and costs.

sections.heading: Duty to review provision for representation of data subjects
sections.title: Mandatory review of data subject representation mechanisms
sections.summary: This section requires the Secretary of State to review data subject representation mechanisms within 30 months of section 187 coming into force. The review covers GDPR Article 80(1), section 187, and the merits of allowing representative bodies to exercise rights without authorization. It specifically addresses children's rights and requires consideration of child development needs and UN Convention on the Rights of the Child.

The powers include creating procedures for collective claims and compensation.

sections.summary: The Secretary of State must lay documents prepared under section 191 before Parliament. If either House rejects the document within 40 days, it cannot be issued. If no rejection occurs, the document is issued and takes effect after 21 days. The 40-day period starts from the day of laying and excludes whole days. This section also applies to amendments prepared under section 191.

Failure to comply does not make a person liable for legal proceedings. The document is admissible in evidence in legal proceedings if relevant to the question. Courts and tribunals must consider the document when determining questions arising in proceedings if relevant.

sections.heading: Reserve forces: data-sharing by HMRC

Disclosure of these details is prohibited without consent from the Commissioners for Her Majesty's Revenue and Customs. Unauthorized disclosure is an offense with specific defenses.

sections.heading: Penalties for offences
sections.title: Penalties for data processing offenses
sections.summary: Offenders under sections 119, 173, 170, 184, etc. face fines on summary conviction in England and Wales. In Scotland and Northern Ireland, fines are limited to statutory maximums. Courts may order destruction of materials used in processing personal data if connected to the offense. Non-offenders must be given opportunity to show why materials should not be destroyed.

sections.heading: Prosecution
sections.title: Prosecution procedures for data offenses
sections.summary: In England and Wales, prosecutions for data offenses may only be initiated by the Commissioner or with DPP consent. In Northern Ireland, prosecutions may only be initiated by the Commissioner or with DPP consent. Summary proceedings for certain offenses must be brought within 6 months of evidence being sufficient, but not later than 3 years after the offense was committed.

sections.heading: Liability of directors etc
sections.title: Director liability for corporate data offences
sections.summary: This section establishes that directors, managers, secretaries, or officers of a body corporate are personally liable for data offences committed by the corporation if the offence was done with their consent, connivance, or due to their neglect. It also extends this liability to members managing the corporation's affairs as if they were directors. The section applies to both English companies and Scottish partnerships.
sections.dtia_summary: Directors and officers face personal liability for data breaches committed by the corporation, which impacts DTIA assessments by requiring explicit risk mitigation for director involvement in data incidents.

sections.heading: Disclosure of information to the Tribunal

The provision remains effective until the repeal of Part 1 of the Regulation of Investigatory Powers Act 2000.

sections.heading: Proceedings in the First-tier Tribunal: contempt
sections.title: Contempt proceedings in First-tier Tribunal
sections.summary: This section defines contempt of the First-tier Tribunal in proceedings related to appeals under specific sections or orders. It allows the First-tier Tribunal to certify such offences to the Upper Tribunal, which may then inquire into the matter and deal with the person charged as it would if the offence occurred in relation to the Upper Tribunal itself. The Upper Tribunal must hear witnesses and statements before exercising its powers.

sections.heading: Meaning of "health professional" and "social work professional"
sections.title: Definitions of health and social work professionals
sections.summary: This section defines 'health professional' and 'social work professional' by listing specific registered practitioners and bodies. It includes medical practitioners, nurses, dentists, optometrists, osteopaths, chiropractors, and others registered under various statutory orders and acts. It also defines 'health service body' to include government bodies, local authorities, NHS trusts, and other health-related organizations.

sections.source_quote: 204 Meaning of "health professional" and "social work professional" (1) In this Act, "health professional" means any of the following— (a) a registered medical practitioner; (b) a registered nurse or midwife; (c) a registered dentist within the meaning of the Dentists Act 1984 (see section 53 of that Act); (d) a registered dispensing optician or a registered optometrist within the meaning of the Opticians Act 1989 (see section 36 of that Act); (e) a registered osteopath with the meaning of the Osteopaths Act 1993 (see section 41 of that Act); (f) a registered chiropractor within the meaning of the Chiropractors Act 1994 (see section 43 of that Act); (g) a person registered as a member of a profession to which the Health Professions Order 2001 (S.I. 2002/254) for the time being extends; (h) a registered pharmacist or a registered pharmacy technician within the meaning of the Pharmacy Order 2010 (S.I. 2010/2 31) (see article 3 of that Order); (i) a registered person within the meaning of the Pharmacy (Northern Ireland) Order 1976 (S.I. 1976/1213 (N.I. 22)) (see Article 2 of that Order); (j) a child psychotherapist; (k) a scientist employed by a health service body as head of a department.

sections.heading: General interpretation

sections.summary: This section defines critical terms used throughout the Data Protection Act 2018, including biometric data, health data, genetic data, government departments, health records, inaccurate data, international obligations, international organisations, and other specific concepts. These definitions establish the precise meaning of terms for legal purposes under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides foundational definitions for data types and concepts that directly impact how data transfers and processing are evaluated in DTIAs, particularly for sensitive data categories.

requirements.text: biometric data means personal data resulting from specific technical processing relating to the physical, physiological or behavioural characteristics of an individual, which allows or confirms the unique identification of that individual
data concerning health means personal data relating to the physical or mental health of an individual, including the provision of health care services
genetic data means personal data relating to the inherited or acquired genetic characteristics of an individual which gives unique information about the physiology or the health of that individual
government department includes the following (except in the expression 'United Kingdom government department')
health record means a record which consists of data concerning health and has been made by or on behalf of a health professional in connection with the diagnosis, care or treatment of the individual
inaccurate, in relation to personal data, means incorrect or misleading as to any matter of fact
international obligation of the United Kingdom includes obligations arising from international agreements

sections.heading: C21
sections.title: Conditions for processing personal data for specific public purposes
sections.summary: This section outlines specific conditions under which processing of personal data is permissible without consent. It covers scenarios where processing is necessary for administration of justice, parliamentary functions, equality of opportunity, racial and ethnic diversity at senior levels, preventing unlawful acts, protecting the public against dishonesty, regulatory compliance, journalism, preventing fraud, and other public interest purposes. The conditions require that processing serves a specific public purpose and meets defined criteria such as necessity, non-discrimination, and minimal impact.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes specific public interest exceptions where data transfers can occur without consent, particularly for law enforcement, public safety, and legitimate government activities. These exceptions require careful assessment of necessity and proportionality to avoid overreach.

sections.title: Disclosure of personal data to elected representatives
sections.summary: This section defines when disclosure of personal data to elected representatives constitutes lawful processing. Disclosure must be for communication from the representative to the data subject, relevant to the communication, and necessary for responding to it. If the request originates from a third party, consent from the data subject is only required if the elected representative cannot reasonably obtain consent, obtaining consent would prejudice their actions, or consent would be unreasonable.

Disclosure is permitted if the other individual has consented or it is reasonable to disclose without consent. Reasonableness is assessed based on information type, confidentiality duties, consent-seeking steps, the other individual's capacity to consent, and any prior refusals. The section defines 'information relating to another individual' and establishes conditions under which disclosure can occur without consent.

sections.title: Transitional provisions for subordinate legislation amendments
sections.summary: This section outlines transitional provisions for subordinate legislation amended by Schedule 19 to the Data Protection Act 2018. It specifies that amendments to the 2002 Act do not affect the determination of whether authorities complied with Part 1 of the 2002 Act. It also clarifies that Scottish Information Commissioner and Court of Session powers remain unchanged regarding compliance with amended legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures continuity in compliance assessments for Scottish authorities when applying amended data protection legislation post-implementation.

requirements.text: Amendments of the 2002 Act in Schedule 19 do not affect determination of compliance with Part 1 of the 2002 Act
Scottish Information Commissioner and Court of Session powers do not include requiring steps beyond compliance with amended Part 1 of the 2002 Act

sections.source_quote: Amendments of the 2002 Act in Schedule 19 do not affect determination of compliance with Part 1 of the 2002 Act

sections.heading: Provision inserted into subordinate legislation by this Schedule may be amended (cont.)
sections.title: List of countries and jurisdictions with adequate data protection
sections.summary: This section specifies countries and jurisdictions that qualify as having adequate data protection for transfers under the UK GDPR. It includes EEA states, Gibraltar, EU institutions, and specific third countries with adequacy decisions from the European Commission prior to IP completion.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines which countries and jurisdictions have adequate data protection for transfers under the UK GDPR, enabling direct transfers without additional safeguards.

requirements.text: EEA states, Gibraltar, and EU institutions are specified as having adequate data protection
Specific third countries with adequacy decisions from the European Commission prior to IP completion are listed
Transfers to countries with adequate protection may rely on those decisions for compliance

sections.source_quote: EEA states, Gibraltar, and EU institutions are specified as having adequate data protection

sections.heading: Provision inserted into subordinate legislation by this Schedule may be amended (cont.)
sections.title: Cross-border transfer approval for law enforcement data
sections.summary: This section establishes that transfers of personal data to third countries or international organizations for law enforcement purposes are treated as approved by regulations if paragraph 11 specifies the destination country or organization. The approval depends on specific conditions outlined in paragraph 11, which may restrict the transfers to particular countries or organizations. The Secretary of State may amend these regulations to repeal or modify the approval conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: Transfers of law enforcement data to third countries require specific regulatory approval based on destination country or organization. The approval is conditional on paragraph 11 specifying the transfer scope, and the Secretary of State can amend these rules to adjust which transfers are approved.

requirements.text: Transfers of personal data to third countries or international organizations for law enforcement purposes are treated as approved by regulations if paragraph 11 specifies the destination country or organization
Paragraph 11 must specify the country or organization for the transfer to be approved
The Secretary of State may repeal sub-paragraphs (1) and (2) and paragraph 11
The Secretary of State may amend paragraph 11 to omit a specified country or organization
The Secretary of State may amend paragraph 11 to replace a reference with a narrower description
Regulations may identify transfers by controller, processor, recipient, data transferred, or means of transfer

sections.heading: Provision inserted into subordinate legislation by this Schedule may be amended (cont.)
sections.title: Amendments to subordinate legislation by SI 2026/386
sections.summary: This section lists multiple provisions in the Data Protection Act 2018 that have been amended by the Statutory Instrument 2026/386. Each amendment affects specific sections of the Act, including substitutions of words and omissions of provisions. The amendments are detailed in the Schedule to the SI, which specifies the exact changes made to the legislation. This section states that provisions inserted into subordinate legislation by this Schedule may be amended. It lists specific sections of the Data Protection Act 2018 that have been amended by the 2026/386 Statutory Instrument. The amendments affect various provisions related to data processing and privacy.

requirements.text: Provisions inserted into subordinate legislation by this Schedule may be amended

sections.heading: Provision inserted into subordinate legislation by this Schedule may be amended (cont.)
sections.title: Amendments to subordinate legislation by Schedule 2
sections.summary: This section lists multiple amendments to subordinate legislation made by the 2026/386 Statutory Instrument. It specifies substitutions and omissions of words in various sections and schedules of the Data Protection Act 2018, including changes to specific provisions and schedules. The amendments are detailed with the exact sections and schedules affected, along with the relevant statutory instruments that implemented the changes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section does not directly impact data transfer impact assessments as it only describes amendments to existing provisions without introducing new transfer mechanisms or requirements.
